source: All-In Podcast — 2025 predictions (bestie guest Gavin Baker)
summary: FSD is crossing into mainstream adoption and Tesla's autonomous driving will compound at an accelerating rate in 2025
summary_extended: Baker argues that Tesla's Full Self-Driving (FSD) technology has crossed a functional threshold and is already influencing real-world consumer behavior, citing personal anecdotes such as preferring Tesla-based Uber rides at night as evidence that FSD 'works today.' He believes 2025 will be the year FSD transitions from early-adopter curiosity to mainstream adoption, with the rate of adoption compounding as more consumers experience the technology firsthand. The investment implication is straightforwardly bullish on Tesla, as accelerating FSD adoption would drive both hardware sales and high-margin software/subscription revenue. Baker does not provide specific price targets, penetration rates, or revenue figures, making this a qualitative conviction call rather than a quantitatively grounded thesis.

source: iConnections Global Alts 2025 — The Future of AI
summary: 2025 is the year of AI applications — software and internet companies will be primary beneficiaries of higher AI ROI
summary_extended: Baker argues that 2025 marks the inflection point where AI value creation shifts from infrastructure buildout to the application layer, meaning software and internet companies become the primary beneficiaries of AI ROI. His reasoning is that as inference costs fall dramatically — validated by the DeepSeek news — the marginal dollar of AI spend increasingly flows to companies deploying AI in products rather than those building training infrastructure. He cites the market reaction on 'DeepSeek bounce day,' where software and internet stocks outperformed, as real-time confirmation of this thesis. The investment implication is to overweight application-layer software and internet platforms that can monetize cheaper AI inference through improved products and higher user engagement, rather than semiconductor or infrastructure names. Baker's view is independently corroborated by collaborator Gracias, who also characterized 2025 as 'the year of the application.'

source: iConnections Global Alts 2025 — The Future of AI
summary: China's DeepSeek achievement raises serious questions about whether the chip embargo has been circumvented or rendered irrelevant
summary_extended: Baker (and co-speaker Gracias) analyze the implications of China's DeepSeek AI model achievement through three possible explanations: (1) DeepSeek genuinely used only H800 chips as claimed, meaning the embargo worked but was insufficient to prevent competitive AI development; (2) the embargo was violated and DeepSeek secretly accessed H100-class chips; or (3) China developed an entirely different compute architecture — whether a novel chip design or quantum-based — that sidesteps the need for leading-edge US semiconductors altogether. Baker identifies Option 3 as the most alarming scenario from a US national security and export control perspective, as it would render the entire embargo strategy moot. This is framed as a geopolitical risk analysis rather than a direct investment thesis, with no certainty assigned to any scenario, though the implications for US semiconductor export policy and the competitive AI landscape are significant.
override_note: All mentions. NVDA/MIXED was wrong (NVDA loses China revenue). Thesis = Western semicap-equipment monopolies protected by export controls.

source: iConnections Global Alts 2025 — The Future of AI
summary: 2025 is the year humanoid robotics becomes credibly real in public perception, with 2026 seeing consumer deployment
summary_extended: Baker predicts that 2025 will be the year humanoid robotics becomes credibly real in public perception — analogous to how ChatGPT made AI tangible for knowledge workers in 2022 — with consumer deployment following in 2026, including a specific personal prediction that he will have a Tesla Optimus robot in his home by 2026. His co-investor Gracias reinforces this framing, citing autonomous driving as a leading indicator that physical AI systems are maturing faster than consensus expects. Baker argues this shift will fundamentally alter the US productivity equation, implying humanoid robots could eventually substitute for or augment human labor at scale. The thesis is directional and medium-term, with 2025 as the credibility inflection point and 2026 as the first consumer deployment wave.

source: iConnections Global Alts 2025 — The Future of AI
summary: Cheaper AI inference via DeepSeek-style efficiency will massively increase AI ROI and accelerate deployment phase
summary_extended: Baker argues that the dramatic reduction in AI inference costs — exemplified by DeepSeek-style efficiency gains that cut the cost of equivalent reasoning tasks from roughly $500 to $100 — will trigger a Jevons Paradox dynamic where cheaper intelligence drives superlinear increases in demand. He contends this shift moves AI from a training and experimentation phase into a true deployment phase where real-world ROI is generated at scale, and that capex mix will rebalance away from training compute toward inference infrastructure. Baker frames this as the core structural reason the market recovered after the DeepSeek-driven selloff, arguing the selloff misread the news as negative when cheaper inference is actually a massive accelerant for AI adoption. The investment implication is that companies positioned to monetize AI at the application and deployment layer — rather than pure training infrastructure plays — stand to benefit most as ROI on AI spend becomes demonstrable and widespread.

source: iConnections Global Alts 2025 — The Future of AI
summary: DeepSeek R1 de-risks AI by revealing two new scaling laws beyond pre-training, extending the AI investment runway 5-10 years
summary_extended: Baker argues that DeepSeek R1 was a major de-risking event for AI investment theses, not a threat, because it empirically revealed two new scaling laws beyond pre-training: reinforcement learning during post-training and test-time compute scaling. Prior to OpenAI's O1/O3 and DeepSeek R1, the AI investment community only had pre-training scaling laws as a mechanistically understood growth driver, and there was meaningful risk that scaling laws were breaking down. By demonstrating that RL post-training and test-time compute are additional, independently scalable dimensions, Baker contends the AI buildout cycle is extended materially — explicitly stating it is 'not ending anytime in the next 5 to 10 years.' The investment implication is that the AI infrastructure and foundation model buildout remains a durable multi-year theme, and the key bear case (scaling exhaustion) has been substantially invalidated.

source: iConnections Global Alts 2025 — The Future of AI
summary: xAI has the highest return on capital in AI data centers among Western companies and will maintain this advantage
summary_extended: Baker and Gracias argue that xAI has the highest return on capital among Western AI data center operators, making it their highest-conviction AI infrastructure investment. They attribute this advantage to Elon Musk's capital efficiency philosophy, drawing a direct parallel to how Musk built the Tesla Gigafactory in China at dramatically lower cost than Western peers. They further argue that DeepSeek-style efficiency improvements in AI model training and inference disproportionately benefit high-ROC operators like xAI, since leaner compute economics amplify the advantage of those who already spend less per unit of output. xAI also benefits from a proprietary data moat via the X/Twitter platform, which provides unique training data unavailable to competitors. The investment implication is a direct long position in xAI, though xAI is currently private and has no US-listed equity ticker, limiting public market expression of this thesis.

source: All-In Podcast — E221: AI cold war, Signalgate, CoreWeave IPO, tariffs
summary: AI agents are the key ROI unlock for AI infrastructure spend; compute will be the rate-limiting factor in agent adoption
summary_extended: Baker argues that AI agents represent the critical ROI unlock for the current wave of AI infrastructure spending, and that if agents become widespread, the return on investment for AI compute — and Nvidia's Blackwell GPUs specifically — will be very high. His core reasoning is that agents are far more compute-intensive than traditional inference workloads, meaning demand for GPUs will dramatically exceed supply once agent adoption scales. However, he identifies compute scarcity as the binding near-to-medium-term constraint: there simply will not be enough compute deployed to support truly widespread agent usage for an extended period. He cites OpenAI's initial pricing of its agent products at $10,000–$20,000 per month as concrete evidence that compute scarcity is already being reflected in pricing, validating the infrastructure investment thesis. The investment implication is that Nvidia, as the dominant supplier of the compute that agents require, is the primary beneficiary of this structural dynamic.

source: All-In Podcast — E221: AI cold war, Signalgate, CoreWeave IPO, tariffs
summary: Export controls on GPUs create a cat-and-mouse dynamic that is ultimately difficult to enforce given the value density of modern GPUs
summary_extended: Baker argues that export controls on high-end GPUs like Nvidia's Blackwell are structurally difficult to enforce because the chips are extraordinarily small and valuable per unit, making smuggling far easier than, say, drug interdiction — a single chip worth tens of thousands of dollars can be concealed trivially. He contends that the cat-and-mouse dynamic between U.S. enforcement and Chinese smuggling networks will persist indefinitely until some form of diplomatic 'grand bargain' is reached, suggesting current controls are not a durable long-term solution. The controls create a theoretical U.S. advantage by slowing Chinese AI development, but simultaneously accelerate Chinese investment in domestic semiconductor self-sufficiency, which Baker frames as an unintended consequence that could erode U.S. chip dominance over time. This is presented as a structural geopolitical observation rather than a direct investment call, with no specific timeframe cited for when a grand bargain might materialize.
override_note: All mentions. NVDA/MIXED was wrong (NVDA loses China revenue). Thesis = Western semicap-equipment monopolies protected by export controls.

source: All-In Podcast — E221: AI cold war, Signalgate, CoreWeave IPO, tariffs
summary: Nvidia's investment in neoclouds like CoreWeave was strategically rational — it diversified the buyer base and reduced hyperscaler pricing power
summary_extended: Baker argues that Nvidia's decision to invest in neoclouds like CoreWeave was a strategically rational move analogous to Intel's historic 'Intel Inside' program — it was about market structure, not revenue inflation. By funding CoreWeave and similar neoclouds, Nvidia deliberately fragmented the buyer base, reducing the pricing and negotiating power of the AWS/Azure/GCP hyperscaler oligopoly, which had been capturing an outsized share of GPU procurement leverage. Baker explicitly states that Nvidia would have sold those GPUs to hyperscalers anyway, so the investments did not inflate revenues; instead, they accelerated GPU deployment via reference architectures and diversified demand. The strategic payoff, in Baker's view, is visible in the fact that the big three hyperscalers' incremental share of Nvidia's revenue declined — a structural outcome Nvidia engineered intentionally. The investment implication is that Nvidia's competitive moat is deeper than it appears, as the company actively manages its customer concentration risk and supply chain influence rather than passively benefiting from AI demand.
override_note: All mentions. He named both CoreWeave and Nebius at Sohn NY 2026.

source: HedgeFundAlpha (writeup) — SK Hynix / HBM pitch
summary: AI's shift from training to inference is driving rapid and accelerating growth in memory demand, particularly HBM.
summary_extended: Baker argues that the AI industry's structural shift from training-dominated workloads to inference (including reasoning models) is the primary driver of accelerating demand for high-bandwidth memory. His reasoning is that inference workloads — especially those involving large reasoning models — require substantially more memory bandwidth and capacity per unit of compute than training, meaning that as inference scales, memory demand grows disproportionately. This makes HBM the critical bottleneck and investment opportunity in the AI infrastructure stack. The investment implication is a long position in HBM producers, with SK Hynix (000660.KS) named explicitly as the core position in his Sohn Montreal pitch, and Micron (MU) as the only US-listed HBM producer that directly benefits from the same structural demand tailwind. Samsung (SSNLF) is the third major HBM producer but is OTC-listed and has faced HBM qualification challenges, making it a weaker implied beneficiary.

source: HedgeFundAlpha (writeup) — SK Hynix / HBM pitch
summary: Reasoning models (advanced inference-time compute scaling) are a structural catalyst for HBM demand growth beyond what consensus expects.
summary_extended: Baker argues that the emergence of reasoning models — which scale compute at inference time rather than only at training time — represents a structural catalyst for HBM demand that the market is materially underestimating. Reasoning models require significantly more memory bandwidth per inference step compared to standard forward-pass models, meaning each inference job consumes far more HBM capacity, driving a step-change in memory intensity per GPU cluster. This implies that consensus HBM demand forecasts, which may be anchored to training-era assumptions, are too low, and that HBM suppliers stand to benefit disproportionately as inference workloads scale. Baker frames this as a reshaping force across the semiconductor landscape, with SK Hynix (the dominant HBM supplier) as the primary beneficiary, and Micron as the only US-listed HBM producer also positioned to capture incremental demand. The investment implication is to be long HBM-exposed memory producers, particularly those with leading HBM3e and HBM4 capacity, as reasoning model adoption accelerates through the medium term.
override_note: Sohn Montreal 2025-05-28: 'reasoning drives HBM demand' -> MU.

source: Invested by Aleph — Semiconductors, China/Taiwan fabs, global warming (China's Not Getting Taiwan's Fabs)
summary: Semiconductors (public and private) are structurally advantaged because AI makes global GDP inexorably more silicon-intensive
summary_extended: Baker argues that semiconductors represent a structurally advantaged investment category because AI fundamentally and permanently increases the silicon intensity of global GDP. His core evidence is a margin/compute-spend comparison: AI companies operate at roughly 40% gross margins with approximately 50% of revenue flowing to compute, versus traditional SaaS businesses at 85% gross margins with only ~5% going to compute — meaning the shift from SaaS to AI-native business models dramatically expands the total addressable market for silicon. This structural dynamic makes it difficult to bet against semiconductors as a category, and Baker expresses conviction across both public and private semiconductor investments. He is particularly enthusiastic about venture-stage semiconductor companies, noting that founders in this space are often experienced operators in their 50s with proven track records, which he views as a quality signal. The investment implication is a broad long bias on the semiconductor sector, with no single company singled out as the primary beneficiary.

source: Invested by Aleph — Semiconductors, China/Taiwan fabs, global warming (China's Not Getting Taiwan's Fabs)
summary: US semiconductor export controls on China are working and have extended the Western lead to potentially multi-decade
summary_extended: Baker argues that US semiconductor export controls on China are working effectively and have dramatically extended the Western technological lead, potentially to a multi-decade timeframe. He explicitly disagrees with Bill Gurley's view that export controls are counterproductive, citing that before controls were implemented China was approximately 6-8 years away from catching up in both semiconductor manufacturing and design, whereas now the gap has widened to roughly 4 years and is likely to continue expanding. His key insight is that semiconductor manufacturing knowledge is tacit and non-transferable — analogous to jet engine technology, which took China the longest to replicate in aviation despite having access to blueprints — meaning that even with stolen plans, China cannot simply copy leading-edge fabs. The investment implication is that Western semiconductor equipment and design companies with dominant positions in the supply chain face no credible Chinese competitive threat for the foreseeable future, reinforcing the durability of their moats and pricing power.
override_note: All mentions. NVDA/MIXED was wrong (NVDA loses China revenue). Thesis = Western semicap-equipment monopolies protected by export controls.

source: Invested by Aleph — Semiconductors, China/Taiwan fabs, global warming (China's Not Getting Taiwan's Fabs)
summary: Scaled foundation model companies are dramatically better investments post-reasoning models due to emergence of data flywheel
summary_extended: Baker argues that the emergence of reasoning models (via RLVF and test-time compute) has fundamentally changed the investment case for scaled foundation model companies by creating a user-data-product flywheel that did not previously exist in standard LLMs. Specifically, he contends that a large user base now directly feeds back into model quality improvement, giving companies with massive user bases a compounding structural advantage that smaller or newer entrants cannot easily replicate. Baker identifies OpenAI, Anthropic, XAI, and Google DeepMind as the primary beneficiaries of this dynamic, viewing them as a durable oligopoly that can now be valued on revenue multiples rather than speculative metrics. The investment implication is that scaled foundation model companies are dramatically better investments post-reasoning than they were before, and that this structural moat makes them long-term holds. Notably, most of the named beneficiaries (OpenAI, Anthropic, XAI) are private, limiting direct public equity expression of this thesis.

theme: Edge AI as bear case for cloud
source: Invested by Aleph — Semiconductors, China/Taiwan fabs, global warming (China's Not Getting Taiwan's Fabs)
summary: Edge AI / on-device AI is a meaningful risk to the centralized data center boom
summary_extended: Baker identifies edge AI / on-device AI as a meaningful structural risk to the current hyperscale data center buildout, framing it explicitly as a downside scenario rather than a base case. He argues that within 2-3 years, a smartphone could run a 130 IQ AI locally that knows the user personally, handles tasks like banking, and only calls out to cloud AI for complex queries — dramatically reducing per-user cloud compute demand. This mirrors the historical oscillation between centralized mainframe computing and decentralized personal computing, suggesting the current centralized AI infrastructure boom may not be permanent. The investment implication is bearish for companies whose valuations depend heavily on sustained hyperscale data center demand growth, particularly those building or monetizing large-scale AI inference infrastructure. Baker does not name specific companies but the risk is most acute for hyperscaler capex beneficiaries like GPU suppliers and cloud infrastructure providers.

source: Invested by Aleph — Semiconductors, China/Taiwan fabs, global warming (China's Not Getting Taiwan's Fabs)
summary: Humanoid robots will be ubiquitous in homes and factories within 10 years
summary_extended: Baker predicts that humanoid robots will be ubiquitous in both homes and factories within a 10-year timeframe, framing this as a high-conviction long-term structural view. He cites the Optimus robot (Tesla's humanoid) mining asteroids as an even longer-term vision, suggesting he sees the trajectory of humanoid robotics extending well beyond near-term industrial use cases. The thesis is presented alongside a prediction about the arrival of artificial superintelligence (ASI), implying Baker views these as complementary mega-trends in the coming decade. However, Baker does not name specific investable companies or provide a detailed mechanism for how to express this view, making the investment implication directionally clear (long humanoid robotics exposure) but not precisely actionable from this excerpt alone.

source: Invested by Aleph — Semiconductors, China/Taiwan fabs, global warming (China's Not Getting Taiwan's Fabs)
summary: Intel has a real path to recovery under Lip-Bu Tan with US government support, and American-made wafers could command ~100% price premium
summary_extended: Baker argues that Intel has a credible path to recovery under new CEO Lip-Bu Tan, whom he describes as a legendary semiconductor investor and executive who genuinely understands what needs to be done — contrasting him favorably with Pat Gelsinger, whom Baker believes was unfairly maligned despite pursuing the only viable strategy. The US government taking an equity stake in Intel signals that it is viewed as a national strategic asset, providing both financial support and political cover for the turnaround. Baker points to TSMC's Arizona fab as proof of concept for the geopolitical premium thesis: Arizona capacity sold out instantly and prices were raised 20% — and it still sold out — suggesting demand for American-made wafers is inelastic at current price levels. Baker believes geopolitical fears around Taiwan are real enough that American-fabricated wafers could command close to a 100% price premium over equivalent offshore production, making Intel's domestic foundry ambitions potentially very valuable if execution improves.

source: Invested by Aleph — Semiconductors, China/Taiwan fabs, global warming (China's Not Getting Taiwan's Fabs)
summary: Nvidia is extremely difficult to displace as the dominant accelerator platform; attempts to out-GPU the GPU are largely a fool's errand
summary_extended: Baker argues that Nvidia is extraordinarily difficult to displace as the dominant AI accelerator platform, characterizing attempts to replicate GPU capabilities head-on as 'a fool's errand' and stating it is 'very hard to out-Nvidia Nvidia.' His reasoning centers on Nvidia's deep systems and software stack (CUDA ecosystem) which competitors cannot easily replicate — AMD, despite being a good company, lacks this integrated stack. Baker uses historical evidence to support the durability of Nvidia's moat: Google's TPU required three full generations before achieving meaningful success, and AWS Trainium — which he considers the best hyperscaler silicon effort — still needs 2-3 more generations to be competitive. The investment implication is a strong long bias on NVDA, with skepticism toward hyperscaler ASIC programs as near-to-medium-term threats, and limited conviction in AMD as a credible GPU alternative.

source: Invested by Aleph — Semiconductors, China/Taiwan fabs, global warming (China's Not Getting Taiwan's Fabs)
summary: SpaceX is the highest risk-adjusted return (Sharpe ratio) asset available in any market, public or private
summary_extended: Baker argues that SpaceX represents the single highest risk-adjusted return (Sharpe ratio) investment available across all public and private markets, making it his highest-conviction idea. His reasoning rests on three pillars: Starlink alone addresses a $1 trillion-plus global telecom market with a product he views as structurally superior to all terrestrial alternatives; SpaceX is the most critical Western defense asset, which he ranks above even Anduril in strategic importance; and the company exhibits 'pro-entropic' characteristics, meaning it becomes more valuable as global disorder increases. Baker also cites long-term optionality by comparing SpaceX's potential trajectory to the British East India Company, implying it could become a dominant commercial and geopolitical force across the solar system. The investment implication is a strong long conviction in SpaceX as a private market holding, with no directly traded public equity proxy he identifies.

source: Invested by Aleph — Semiconductors, China/Taiwan fabs, global warming (China's Not Getting Taiwan's Fabs)
summary: XAI and Google have the most unique data among frontier labs, giving them structural training advantages
summary_extended: Baker argues that among frontier AI labs, xAI and Google possess the most structurally defensible data moats, giving them durable training advantages over competitors. His reasoning is that xAI has exclusive access to the X platform's real-time social data stream, while Google benefits from its unmatched search index and knowledge graph — both representing data assets that cannot be replicated by rivals. This view represents a refinement of his prior thesis that foundation models lacking unique proprietary data are the fastest-depreciating assets in technology. Baker notes that Anthropic made a smart early bet on code-focused training data, and OpenAI secured internet-scale distribution by being first to market, but neither has the same quality of unique, ongoing data generation as xAI or Google. The investment implication is that GOOGL is the primary publicly-listed beneficiary of this structural data moat thesis, as xAI remains private.

source: a16z Podcast — Is there an AI bubble?
summary: No AI bubble today — GPU utilization vs. dark fiber is the key contrast to 2000
summary_extended: Baker argues that there is no AI bubble today, drawing a sharp contrast to the 2000 telecom bubble where 97% of fiber laid was dark (unused), whereas today every GPU is being utilized at or beyond capacity — sometimes to the point of melting during training runs. He supports this view with valuation data: Nvidia currently trades at approximately 40x trailing earnings versus Cisco's 150-180x at the peak of the 2000 bubble, suggesting far more reasonable pricing relative to the prior bubble analog. Further evidence comes from hyperscaler return on invested capital, which has increased roughly 10 percentage points since they began ramping AI capex, confirming that the spend is generating positive ROI rather than speculative waste. The investment implication is that AI infrastructure spending — particularly in GPUs and datacenter buildout — remains fundamentally justified, and that Nvidia specifically is not in bubble territory on a valuation basis relative to historical precedents.

source: a16z Podcast — Is there an AI bubble?
summary: Hyperscaler capex ROI has been strongly positive; debate is whether it continues with Blackwell ramp
summary_extended: Baker argues that the ROI on hyperscaler AI capex has been unambiguously positive to date, citing roughly 10-point increases in ROIC across the major public cloud and AI spenders since they began ramping GPU infrastructure. He contends that the 'AI bubble' debate is largely misframed because the financial returns are already visible in the numbers, not speculative. Baker personally believes ROI will remain positive through the Blackwell buildout cycle, though he acknowledges this forward-looking view is genuinely contested. As a collective buffer against capex risk, he notes these hyperscalers generate approximately $300B in annual free cash flow and hold ~$500B in cash, creating an ~$800B cushion that limits downside even if returns compress. The investment implication is to remain long the major hyperscalers as a group, with the key watch item being whether Blackwell-era spending sustains the ROIC trajectory.

source: a16z Podcast — Is there an AI bubble?
summary: Most custom ASIC programs will fail within 3 years; Google TPU external sales could be the catalyst
summary_extended: Baker argues that the vast majority of custom ASIC programs being pursued by hyperscalers will fail within three years, making this a high-conviction contrarian call against the prevailing narrative that in-house silicon will displace Nvidia. His reasoning is that custom silicon is extraordinarily difficult to execute — Google itself required three full generations of iteration before its TPU became viable — and most hyperscalers lack the institutional knowledge and engineering depth to replicate that journey. Baker specifically singles out Broadcom's go-to-market strategy of pairing custom ASICs with an Ethernet fabric as an alternative to Nvidia's NVLink/InfiniBand stack, expressing skepticism that this approach will succeed at scale. The investment implication is that Nvidia remains the dominant AI accelerator vendor by default, while AMD persists only as a necessary second-source; Google's potential external TPU sales could be the key catalyst that exposes the weakness of competing ASIC programs by offering a credible third option that most hyperscalers still cannot match internally. Broadcom is identified as the primary company whose ASIC business faces execution risk, while Nvidia is the structural beneficiary if custom ASIC programs broadly fail.
override_note: All mentions. PAIR_TRADE: long NVDA+GOOGL / short AVGO. No returns computed.

source: a16z Podcast — Is there an AI bubble?
summary: Nvidia's roundtripping investments in AI labs are strategically rational competitive responses to Google's TPU ecosystem
summary_extended: Baker argues that Nvidia's equity investments in AI labs like OpenAI and xAI — often criticized as 'roundtripping' where Nvidia invests cash that comes back as chip purchases — are strategically rational competitive responses rather than financial engineering. His core reasoning is that Google funds Anthropic with both chips and capital, creating a bundled competitive offering that locks in Anthropic as a TPU customer; Nvidia must mirror this strategy to retain OpenAI and xAI as GPU customers or risk losing them to Google's ecosystem. Baker dismisses roundtripping concerns as 'pretty overblown,' framing every move Jensen Huang has made as completely rational long-term strategy, and notes that Huang is one of the two best CEOs he has ever encountered. The investment implication is that NVDA's balance sheet deployments into AI labs should be viewed as customer retention and competitive moat-building rather than as a red flag, making the stock more attractive than the roundtripping narrative suggests. No specific financial figures or timeframes are cited, but the medium-term horizon implies Baker expects this competitive dynamic to persist through the current AI infrastructure buildout cycle.

source: a16z Podcast — Is there an AI bubble?
summary: Robotics will be Tesla vs. Chinese competitors — humanoid form factor debate is over
summary_extended: Baker argues that humanoid robotics is a genuine and significant opportunity, and that the debate over humanoid versus non-humanoid form factors is definitively settled in favor of humanoids. His reasoning is that humanoids can leverage the vast corpus of human motion data — learning from YouTube videos and demonstrations via motion capture suits — giving them a compounding learning advantage over purpose-built non-humanoid robots. He frames the competitive landscape as mirroring the EV dynamic: Tesla as the leading Western player facing intense competition from Chinese manufacturers, suggesting a duopolar structure rather than a single winner. Optimus-specific videos are reportedly impressing professional roboticists, which Baker cites as a signal of genuine technical progress rather than hype. The investment implication is a long position in Tesla as the primary Western-listed humanoid robotics play, with awareness that Chinese competitors (largely not US-listed) will be formidable.

source: a16z Podcast — Is there an AI bubble?
summary: Consumer AI business model will shift to outcome-based affiliate fees, squeezing out Google's advertising inefficiency premium
summary_extended: Baker argues that the dominant business model for consumer AI assistants will shift from advertising to outcome-based affiliate fees, fundamentally disrupting Google's core search advertising franchise. His reasoning is that Google's advertising model generates excess returns precisely because advertisers systematically overpay for customer acquisition — a structural inefficiency that AI can eliminate by closing the loop between intent and transaction (e.g., an AI assistant that books a hotel on a user's behalf and earns a fee only upon completion). This shift would compress the 'inefficiency premium' embedded in Google's ad revenue, representing a meaningful degradation of its business model over time. Baker notes that his personal preferred AI assistant is XAI/Grok, which he discloses is influenced by his direct shareholding in XAI, though XAI is not publicly listed. The investment implication is bearish on GOOGL's long-term advertising revenue durability, though Baker hedges on timing.

source: a16z Podcast — Is there an AI bubble?
summary: Application SaaS companies must accept gross margin compression to succeed in AI — avoiding it is an existential mistake
summary_extended: Baker argues that it is 'definitionally impossible' for application SaaS companies to succeed in AI while preserving their current 80-90% gross margins, because the compute intensity of AI inference and the economics of scaling laws structurally require higher cost of goods sold. He draws an explicit parallel to Microsoft and Adobe's cloud transitions, where both companies accepted meaningful gross margin compression during the shift yet went on to be exceptional stocks — suggesting the market should reward, not punish, AI-driven margin compression. His contrarian warning is directed at SaaS companies that are trying to protect legacy gross margin profiles by under-investing in AI compute, which he frames as the same category error retailers made when dismissing Amazon's low-margin e-commerce model as unsustainable. The investment implication is that investors should not screen out or penalize SaaS companies for gross margin compression if that compression is driven by AI adoption and scaling — rather, it should be read as a signal of competitive seriousness. Baker does not name specific SaaS companies as buy or sell targets; the thesis is a sector-wide structural framework about how to interpret AI-era income statements.

source: a16z Podcast — Is there an AI bubble?
summary: GPT-5 references to scaling law failure are misguided — it was designed for economics, not capability
summary_extended: Baker argues forcefully that the market's interpretation of GPT-5 as evidence of scaling law failure is fundamentally mistaken. He contends that GPT-5 was deliberately designed as a smaller, more economical model optimized for cost efficiency and commercial deployment economics — not as a frontier capability model intended to push the boundaries of AI performance. Because GPT-5 was never meant to be a capability benchmark, drawing any conclusions about the health or limits of scaling laws from its performance is, in Baker's words, 'crazy.' The investment implication is that scaling laws remain intact, and the broader AI infrastructure buildout thesis — including continued demand for high-end compute — should not be derailed by GPT-5 narratives. Baker does not cite specific numbers but is high-conviction and dismissive of the consensus bearish read on scaling.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — GPUs, TPUs, economics of AI (Nvidia vs Google)
summary: AI ROI is empirically positive and unambiguous based on ROIC data from public GPU spenders
summary_extended: Baker argues that the ROI debate around AI spending is empirically settled and 'strange' given publicly available data: the largest GPU buyers are public companies with audited financials, and their ROIC is measurably higher now than before they ramped GPU capex. He identifies two primary drivers of this ROIC improvement — opex savings from AI automation and the migration of recommendation and advertising systems from CPUs to GPUs, which has driven both efficiency gains and revenue acceleration. As a cautionary counterpoint, Baker notes that Meta's ROIC declined in the one quarter it failed to produce a frontier model, underscoring that continued AI investment and capability advancement is necessary to sustain these returns. The investment implication is that the largest hyperscaler GPU spenders — companies with the scale to deploy AI across advertising, cloud, and consumer products — are generating real, auditable returns, making the bear case on AI capex ROI empirically weak. Baker frames this as a structural, ongoing dynamic rather than a one-time event, suggesting continued GPU spending by these companies is justified and likely.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — GPUs, TPUs, economics of AI (Nvidia vs Google)
summary: Anthropic is burning dramatically less cash than OpenAI while growing faster, benefiting from Google/Amazon TPU and Trainium access
summary_extended: Baker argues that Anthropic is demonstrating superior capital efficiency relative to OpenAI, burning dramatically less cash while growing faster, which he attributes in large part to its privileged access to Google TPUs and Amazon Trainium chips — compute infrastructure that carries lower cost than Nvidia GPUs. He notes that Anthropic's cloud partnerships with Google and Amazon effectively give it the same low-cost compute advantages that Google enjoys internally, structurally advantaging it versus OpenAI which relies more heavily on expensive Nvidia hardware. Baker views Anthropic's recent $5B deal with Nvidia as strategically significant, interpreting it as a signal that CEO Dario Amodei believes Blackwell and future Rubin GPUs will be superior to TPUs and that Anthropic is hedging its compute stack accordingly. The investment implication is not a direct public equity play on Anthropic itself (private), but Baker's framing implicitly credits Google and Amazon as infrastructure beneficiaries whose TPU and Trainium platforms are enabling a credible, capital-efficient challenger to OpenAI.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — GPUs, TPUs, economics of AI (Nvidia vs Google)
summary: China made a strategic error by restricting Blackwell access; the gap between American frontier labs and Chinese AI will widen significantly
summary_extended: Baker argues that China made a strategic geopolitical error by restricting its AI labs' access to Nvidia Blackwell GPUs, forcing them to rely on inferior domestic alternatives like Huawei Ascend chips. He cites DeepSeek's v3.2 paper as implicit acknowledgment that compute scarcity is a meaningful competitive disadvantage for Chinese labs, suggesting the gap is already widening. As Blackwell deploys at scale across American frontier labs, Baker contends the capability differential will compound dramatically, with China likely recognizing the mistake only in late 2026 — by which point American labs will have built durable, compounding advantages. The investment implication is that Nvidia is the direct beneficiary, as American labs will continue to aggressively procure Blackwell GPUs while Chinese demand is structurally suppressed, concentrating the most advanced compute spending in the US. Baker also dismisses the rare earth leverage narrative, arguing alternatives are being developed rapidly, reducing China's retaliatory options.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — GPUs, TPUs, economics of AI (Nvidia vs Google)
summary: Most ASICs other than Trainium and TPU will fail; only those with multi-generation experience will be competitive
summary_extended: Baker argues that the ASIC market for AI accelerators will consolidate to only two viable players — Google's TPU and Amazon's Trainium — because building a competitive ASIC requires at least three chip generations of accumulated learning, a bar that almost no other company can meet. He supports this with the historical example of Google's TPU, which was not competitive until v3/v4, and notes that Amazon's Trainium 3 is the first generation that is merely 'okay,' with Trainium 4 likely to be genuinely good. Baker explicitly states he will be surprised if any ASICs beyond Trainium and TPU succeed, implying that all other ASIC efforts — from startups and hyperscalers alike — will fail to achieve competitive scale. Compounding this, Nvidia's annual GPU cadence raises the full-stack competitive bar continuously, making it structurally harder for any new ASIC entrant to catch up. The investment implication is that Nvidia remains the dominant AI accelerator beneficiary by default, while Amazon and Google are the only credible internal ASIC challengers, and AMD and other GPU/ASIC competitors are unlikely to displace this dynamic.

theme: Edge AI as bear case for cloud
source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — GPUs, TPUs, economics of AI (Nvidia vs Google)
summary: Edge AI running pruned frontier models on-device is the primary bear case for cloud AI compute demand
summary_extended: Baker identifies edge AI — running pruned frontier models locally on-device — as the most plausible and serious bear case for cloud AI compute demand, second only to scaling laws breaking down. He argues that within three years, smartphones (likely larger and carrying more on-device DRAM) will be capable of running pruned versions of models like Gemini 5 at 30–60 tokens per second at zero marginal cost to the user. Apple's explicit stated strategy is to act as an AI distributor: handle as much inference as possible privately on-device and call out to cloud 'god models' only when local capability is insufficient. Baker's investment implication is cautionary for cloud inference beneficiaries — if on-device intelligence reaches roughly 115 IQ and proves 'good enough' for most tasks, demand for cloud inference compute (and thus GPU/TPU datacenter buildout) could materially disappoint relative to current expectations.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — GPUs, TPUs, economics of AI (Nvidia vs Google)
summary: Google has a temporary but significant cost advantage as the lowest-cost token producer due to TPU v6/v7 while Blackwell was delayed
summary_extended: Baker argues that Google gained a temporary but strategically significant cost advantage in AI inference by training Gemini 3 on TPU v6/v7 while Nvidia's Blackwell GPU generation suffered delays, making Google the lowest-cost token producer in the market during this window. He frames TPU v6/v7 as analogous to an 'F4 Phantom' — a highly capable, battle-tested platform — while Blackwell is the 'F-35,' more powerful but delayed, leaving Google with a meaningful head start. Baker contends this cost advantage is not accidental but reflects a deliberate Google strategy to 'suck the economic oxygen out of the AI ecosystem' by offering AI services at negative margins, thereby starving competitors and startups of the revenue needed to sustain their own model development and infrastructure investment. The investment implication is that Google's vertical integration via TPUs gives it a structural lever to compete on cost that pure-play model companies and cloud rivals relying on Nvidia hardware cannot easily replicate in the near term. Baker views this as a profound and underappreciated dynamic that could reshape competitive positioning across the AI stack.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — GPUs, TPUs, economics of AI (Nvidia vs Google)
summary: Nuclear and quantum computing public equities are in bubble territory; the best players are not publicly investable
summary_extended: Baker argues that publicly traded nuclear/SMR/fusion and quantum computing stocks are in clear bubble territory, with valuations disconnected from fundamentals. His core reasoning is that the actual leaders in quantum computing — Google, IBM, and Honeywell Quantum — are not pure-play public quantum equities but rather large diversified companies where quantum is a minor division, meaning retail investors cannot get clean exposure to the real leaders. Baker explicitly states that none of the public pure-play vehicles are good expressions of the quantum or nuclear theme, and that quantum supremacy is widely misunderstood: quantum computers can perform certain calculations classical computers cannot, but they do not broadly replace classical computing. The investment implication is to avoid the pure-play public quantum and nuclear/SMR stocks, which Baker calls 'really clear bubbles,' rather than to buy the large-cap companies that happen to have quantum programs.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — GPUs, TPUs, economics of AI (Nvidia vs Google)
summary: Power scarcity is structurally bullish for the most compute-efficient chips (Nvidia GPUs), making price irrelevant when watts are the binding constraint
summary_extended: Baker argues that power scarcity fundamentally changes the economics of AI compute procurement, making tokens-per-watt the only metric that matters rather than total cost of ownership or upfront capex. When a data center is power-constrained, the operator should deploy the most compute-efficient chip available regardless of price, because a superior chip generating $25B in revenue from a fixed power envelope beats a cheaper ASIC generating only $8B from the same envelope. This structural dynamic gives maximum pricing power to whoever produces the most advanced, compute-efficient chips — which Baker identifies as Nvidia — for as long as power remains scarce. Baker calls this insight 'really important to me as an investor,' signaling high conviction, and the investment implication is that Nvidia's pricing power is durable and structurally protected by the power constraint rather than being vulnerable to cheaper ASIC alternatives. The thesis implicitly argues against the bear case that custom ASICs from hyperscalers will erode Nvidia's margins, because efficiency-per-watt, not cost-per-chip, is the binding competitive dimension.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — GPUs, TPUs, economics of AI (Nvidia vs Google)
summary: Data centers in space are superior to Earth-based data centers on every first-principles dimension and represent the most important development in the next 3-4 years
summary_extended: Baker argues that space-based data centers are superior to Earth-based data centers on every first-principles dimension and represent the most important infrastructure development of the next 3-4 years. His reasoning rests on four structural advantages: space receives 6x more usable solar energy (30% more intense sunlight with 24-hour exposure and no battery storage required), cooling is essentially free via radiators facing near-absolute-zero space, inter-rack networking via laser through vacuum is faster than fiber, and direct-to-cell satellite connectivity eliminates terrestrial network hops. Baker identifies SpaceX Starship as the only economically viable launch vehicle to make this feasible at scale, and sees Tesla, SpaceX, and xAI converging around this orbital compute infrastructure theme. The investment implication centers on entities connected to Starship and low-Earth-orbit compute buildout, though most of the direct beneficiaries (SpaceX, xAI) are private; Tesla is the only publicly traded company Baker explicitly names in this convergence thesis.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — GPUs, TPUs, economics of AI (Nvidia vs Google)
summary: Reasoning models have created a data flywheel for frontier AI labs that is beginning to generate increasing returns to scale, widening the moat of the top 4 labs
summary_extended: Baker argues that reasoning models have fundamentally transformed the competitive dynamics of frontier AI labs by creating a data flywheel that generates increasing returns to scale. Prior to reasoning models, AI development had no feedback loop — models were static after pre-training and could not improve from user interactions. With reasoning, verifiable outcomes enable Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), meaning each user interaction generates training signal, and each lab's most advanced internal checkpoint is used to train the next generation model, compounding separation between leaders and followers. Baker believes this dynamic is widening the moat of the top four frontier labs (implicitly OpenAI, Google DeepMind, Anthropic, and Meta) and that Blackwell-era compute will further entrench American frontier labs by making it harder for Chinese open-source models to serve as bootstrapping tools for challengers. The investment implication is to be long the top frontier labs with access to this flywheel, with Meta being the only publicly traded pure-play among the four.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — GPUs, TPUs, economics of AI (Nvidia vs Google)
summary: SaaS companies are making an existential mistake by refusing to embrace AI agent economics at lower gross margins
summary_extended: Baker argues that incumbent SaaS companies are making an existential, potentially fatal strategic error by refusing to offer AI agent capabilities at lower gross margins (35-40%), prioritizing the preservation of their current 70-90% gross margin structures instead. He draws a direct historical parallel to brick-and-mortar retailers who refused to invest in e-commerce because it would dilute margins — a decision that proved catastrophic for many of them. Baker contends the correct playbook is for SaaS companies to launch AI agents at 10-20% take rates, leveraging their existing proprietary customer data as a moat, before AI-native competitors undercut and displace them entirely. He states it is 'an absolute guarantee' of failure if SaaS incumbents protect their 80% margins rather than cannibalize themselves, calling it a 'life or death decision,' and singles out Microsoft as the only major SaaS incumbent that is executing this transition correctly. The investment implication is that SaaS companies failing to make this shift — including Salesforce, ServiceNow, HubSpot, GitLab, and Atlassian — face structural disruption risk, while Microsoft is positioned as the relative winner among legacy enterprise software players.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — GPUs, TPUs, economics of AI (Nvidia vs Google)
summary: Gemini 3 confirmed pre-training scaling laws remain intact, which is bullish for next-generation model quality on Blackwell hardware
summary_extended: Baker argues that Gemini 3 served as the first meaningful validation of pre-training scaling laws since the Hopper generation, and the fact that those laws held is a foundational confirmation for the entire AI capital expenditure bull case. He contends this is not merely additive but multiplicative in effect, because it compounds with two newly established post-training scaling laws — RLVR and test-time compute — meaning models trained on Blackwell hardware will be dramatically more capable than those trained on Hopper. The investment implication is straightforwardly bullish for Nvidia, as sustained pre-training scaling laws justify continued and accelerating demand for the most advanced GPU clusters, with Blackwell being the direct beneficiary of this validation. Baker frames Gemini 3's confirmation as removing a key uncertainty that had hung over the AI infrastructure thesis, making the case for sustained hyperscaler GPU procurement significantly stronger.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — GPUs, TPUs, economics of AI (Nvidia vs Google)
summary: Tesla, SpaceX, and XAI are converging into a mutually reinforcing competitive ecosystem
summary_extended: Baker observes that Tesla, SpaceX, and XAI are forming a mutually reinforcing competitive flywheel under Elon Musk's umbrella, with each entity strengthening the others in compounding ways. Specifically, XAI provides the intelligence layer for Tesla's Optimus humanoid robots, Tesla Vision contributes perception capabilities, and SpaceX is positioned to provide space-based data center infrastructure for XAI and Tesla AI workloads. Additionally, XAI benefits from built-in enterprise customer bases via Tesla and SpaceX for deploying AI agents in customer support and sales functions. Baker characterizes this convergence as 'interesting' and 'neat,' citing Musk's own public statements about the strategy, though his conviction is observational rather than a strong actionable call — the implication is that TSLA is the only publicly traded equity in this ecosystem and thus the primary investable expression of the thesis.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — GPUs, TPUs, economics of AI (Nvidia vs Google)
summary: TSMC is being too conservative in capacity expansion, acting as a governor on the AI buildout but potentially making a strategic mistake
summary_extended: Baker argues that TSMC is making a strategic mistake by expanding semiconductor manufacturing capacity too conservatively, acting as a bottleneck on the broader AI infrastructure buildout. He cites TSMC's dismissal of Sam Altman's compute demand projections as evidence of their overly cautious stance, driven by fear of a repeat overbuild cycle. The investment implication is twofold: TSMC's restraint creates an opening for Intel under new CEO Lip-Bu Tan, whose empty fabs could eventually be filled as compute shortages persist and customers seek alternative foundry capacity. Baker acknowledges the tension in his view — TSMC's caution may also serve as a 'natural governor' preventing a speculative bubble — but his base case is that demand will prove TSMC's conservatism wrong over the medium term, making Intel a potential beneficiary of the supply gap.

summary: XAI will release the first Blackwell-trained model in early 2026, giving it a significant competitive advantage
summary_extended: Baker argues that XAI will be the first AI lab to release a model trained on Nvidia's Blackwell GPU architecture, likely in early 2026, giving it a meaningful competitive advantage over rivals. His reasoning rests on Jensen Huang's public statement that no one builds data centers faster than Elon Musk, combined with XAI's demonstrated speed of infrastructure deployment at its Memphis 'Colossus' cluster. Baker emphasizes that receiving Blackwell hardware is only the beginning — it takes an additional 6-9 months to optimize performance on the new architecture, meaning XAI's head start compounds over time and is not easily replicated quickly by competitors. The investment implication is that XAI's frontier model position strengthens materially in 2026, though XAI is not publicly listed; the clearest public-market beneficiary is Nvidia (NVDA) as the supplier of the Blackwell GPUs central to this thesis. Baker calls this 'an important moment' for XAI's competitive standing among frontier AI labs.

source: iConnections Global Alts 2026 — AI, Tesla, defense, energy — what comes next
summary: Power and wafer shortages will prevent an AI overbuild, enabling a smoother and longer AI investment cycle
summary_extended: Baker argues that the AI investment cycle will be structurally different from prior technology bubbles (railroads, internet) because physical constraints — specifically shortages of watts (energy/power) and wafers (TSMC manufacturing capacity) — will prevent the overbuild and subsequent crash that historically follows speculative technology booms. Drawing on Carlotta Perez's framework of technology deployment cycles, he contends that these supply-side bottlenecks act as a natural governor on capital misallocation, forcing a more measured pace of AI infrastructure buildout. The investment implication is that AI infrastructure spending will be sustained and less volatile over a longer horizon than typical bubble cycles, making the sector structurally attractive for long-term investors. Baker frames this as a core conviction view, not a near-term trade, and the thesis is explicitly bullish on the companies that sit at the chokepoints of watts and wafers — power infrastructure and leading-edge semiconductor fabrication.

source: iConnections Global Alts 2026 — AI, Tesla, defense, energy — what comes next
summary: Anthropic is ~4x more capital efficient than OpenAI and growing fast with low cash burn
summary_extended: Baker argues that Anthropic is roughly four times more capital efficient than OpenAI, growing rapidly while consuming relatively little cash, making it a structurally superior AI lab from an investment standpoint. He supports this claim by noting that Anthropic's models can complete comparable-quality tasks using approximately half the tokens of competitors such as Google, which translates directly into lower inference costs and higher margins at scale. This efficiency advantage compounds over time: as AI workloads grow, Anthropic's cost structure allows it to reinvest less capital to maintain competitive parity, widening the gap versus more capital-intensive rivals. The investment implication is primarily relevant to private market investors, as Anthropic is not publicly listed; there is no directly actionable US-listed equity play implied by this specific thesis. Baker presents this as a high-conviction factual observation rather than speculation, grounding it in a specific ratio and a concrete technical mechanism (token efficiency).

source: iConnections Global Alts 2026 — AI, Tesla, defense, energy — what comes next
summary: Humanoid robots will decisively win over specialized robots; Tesla vs. Chinese competitors is the key battle
summary_extended: Baker argues that the debate between humanoid and specialized robots is now definitively settled in favor of humanoid robots, driven by the breakthrough of learning from human video data — a development he views as a decisive inflection point. He frames the competitive landscape as a two-horse race between Tesla and Chinese players, drawing an explicit analogy to the EV market where China emerged as a formidable competitor. Baker believes Tesla holds a meaningful AI advantage in this race, citing its vast real-world driving and robotics data as a structural moat. He views US dominance in humanoid robotics as a national security imperative, suggesting government support or policy tailwinds could further entrench Tesla's position. The investment implication is a long position in Tesla as the primary US-listed pure-play on humanoid robotics, with no other US-listed competitors offering comparable exposure to this specific thesis.

summary: Space-based compute (racks in space connected by laser) is a viable and strategically important architecture
summary_extended: Baker outlines a specific technical vision for space-based compute in which scaled Starlink V3 satellites — each generating approximately 100kW of power — are connected by laser inter-satellite links to form a distributed orbital data center. He argues that lasers operating in vacuum propagate faster than signals through terrestrial fiber, enabling lower latency than any ground-based cloud architecture, and that this latency advantage could command pricing premiums analogous to (or exceeding) the ~70% premium cloud providers already charge for proximity-based compute. Baker frames this architecture as strategically critical for US competitiveness, suggesting sovereign and defense use cases beyond commercial cloud. The investment implication is long-term and speculative: if orbital compute becomes viable, SpaceX (via Starlink) would be the primary infrastructure provider, but SpaceX remains private, leaving no clean US-listed pure-play equity expression of this thesis at present.

summary: SaaS/software companies face existential risk from AI-enabled vibe coding and autonomous software development
summary_extended: Baker argues that AI-enabled 'vibe coding' and autonomous software development tools represent an existential threat to traditional SaaS and pure-play software companies, as non-developers can now build functional software (e.g., a full CRM) in 2-3 hours without writing code. He cites HubSpot's collapse from ~$850 to the low $200s as early evidence of this structural repricing, and notes he has not invested in a pure software company in 6-7 years. The investment implication is to avoid or short pure-play SaaS vendors, particularly those selling point-solution software that can be replicated quickly with AI tools. Baker also identifies a secondary opportunity: AI labs acquiring small-cap software companies cheaply, gutting headcount, and using them as distribution channels — analogous to Larry Ellison's post-dot-com acquisition playbook — suggesting potential M&A activity in beaten-down software names.

source: Capital Allocators — Crossover investing
summary: AI is a multi-decade investment cycle analogous to the early internet, with ChatGPT representing only year three of a 20-30 year trend
summary_extended: Baker argues that AI represents a generational, multi-decade investment cycle analogous to the early internet, explicitly comparing ChatGPT's current moment to Netscape Navigator's role in the mid-1990s — suggesting we are only in year three of a 20-30 year trend. His reasoning draws on historical precedent: just as the internet took decades to fully reshape the economy, AI will similarly compound in importance and economic impact over an extended horizon. The investment implication is that the AI stack — infrastructure, software, and applications — represents a sustained, long-duration opportunity rather than a near-term trade, and Baker frames this view as foundational to his firm-building strategy at Atreides. He does not cite specific companies in this thesis, treating it as a macro-structural framing rather than a stock-specific call. Investors should resist the temptation to time or trade around AI and instead maintain long-duration exposure across the AI ecosystem.

source: Capital Allocators — Crossover investing
summary: Crossover investing (public + private) is a structural advantage in AI because competitors exist at every stack layer in both public and private markets simultaneously
summary_extended: Baker argues that crossover investing — holding both public and private equities simultaneously — is a structural necessity in AI, unlike prior technology waves such as smartphones or SaaS where competition was predominantly among public companies. In AI, every layer of the stack features both public and private competitors: frontier models pit OpenAI, Anthropic, and xAI against Google; semiconductors and applications show the same pattern. Because of this, Baker contends that an investor cannot properly underwrite a public AI company without understanding its private competitors, and cannot underwrite a private AI company without understanding the public landscape — making the crossover lens 'paramount' in AI. The investment implication is structural and process-oriented rather than stock-specific: firms with crossover capabilities have an analytical edge in AI investing that pure public or pure private investors lack. No specific price targets, timeframes, or financial metrics are cited; the thesis is about investor positioning and analytical framework rather than a directional bet on any single company.

source: Sohn NY 2026 — Tech investing fireside
summary: Coding/code generation is not just the killer app for AI but may be the ultimate AI application and the shortest path to AGI
summary_extended: Baker argues that code generation is not merely the 'killer app' for AI but may be the single most important AI application and the most direct path to AGI/ASI, because writing code to autonomously accomplish tasks creates a self-reinforcing loop that accelerates intelligence development. He attributes the original framing to a post on X but endorses it strongly, contending that coding subsumes an ever-expanding range of applications over time as more domains become expressible in code. Practically, Baker recommends that even non-coders use tools like Anthropic's Claude Code and OpenAI's Codex to extract investment insights, suggesting these are already meaningfully useful today. The thesis is structural and long-term in nature, and Baker does not frame it as a specific portfolio position tied to a named equity. No specific financial metrics or timeframes are cited, making this primarily a thematic/directional view rather than an actionable single-stock call.

source: HedgeFundAlpha (writeup) — Baker & Khaira fireside writeup
summary: Memory cycle dynamics represent the most important investment judgment call Baker has faced in his career
summary_extended: Baker explicitly described the memory cycle thesis as the most important investment judgment call of his entire 25+ year career as a technology investor, signaling extraordinarily high conviction that a major inflection point in DRAM and HBM supply-demand dynamics is imminent or underway. While the public writeup does not disclose his directional view (bullish or bearish), the framing implies he sees a structural shift — likely related to the intersection of AI datacenter HBM demand and traditional DRAM cycle dynamics — that is not yet fully priced into markets. The investment implication is that memory-exposed equities, particularly those with significant HBM or DRAM exposure, represent a high-stakes positioning opportunity. summary: Neo/specialist clouds (CoreWeave, Crusoe, Nebius, Lambda) represent a durable business model, not a transitional arbitrage
summary_extended: Baker argues that neo/specialist cloud providers — companies like CoreWeave, Crusoe, Nebius, and Lambda — represent a structurally durable business model rather than a transitional arbitrage that will eventually be displaced by hyperscalers. His core reasoning is that running GPU clusters at high utilization is operationally extremely difficult, analogous to piloting a Formula 1 car, and that CoreWeave in particular achieves 2–3x higher GPU utilization per hour than lower-quality competitors, constituting a genuine and defensible operational moat. He contends that hyperscalers were historically locked in a cost-minimization mentality and are only now beginning the cultural shift required to compete effectively in this space, meaning the window for specialist operators remains open. Atreides holds a disclosed large position in Crusoe, and Baker notes he was conflicted out of CoreWeave at a $1.1B valuation, underscoring his long-term conviction in the category.
