source: Sohn NY 2026 — Tech investing fireside
summary: TSMC's deliberate capacity restraint will prevent an AI bubble and sustain a prolonged upcycle in leading-edge wafer supply
summary_extended: Baker argues that TSMC is deliberately constraining leading-edge wafer capacity expansion far below the pace that Nvidia's Jensen Huang demands, and that this intentional restraint is the key structural difference between the current AI upcycle and prior technology bubbles. He characterizes TSMC's leadership as stewards of Morris Chang's legacy — 'flinty old men and women' — who expand capacity at roughly 5% when Huang visits every three months requesting doublings or triplings. Because physical wafer supply cannot be conjured quickly, Baker contends this bottleneck acts as a natural governor on speculative overinvestment, preventing the kind of bubble that unconstrained capital allocation would otherwise produce. The investment implication is that the AI infrastructure upcycle will be prolonged and structurally sound rather than bubble-prone, and that TSMC as the sole supplier of leading-edge capacity is the critical chokepoint — and thus a core long. Baker notes that Nvidia alone could sell $1–2 trillion of chips if TSMC capacity were available, underscoring the magnitude of suppressed demand flowing through TSM's fabs.

source: HedgeFundAlpha (writeup) — Baker & Khaira fireside writeup
summary: Amazon's Trainium chips are the most underestimated AI play in the market
summary_extended: Baker argues that Amazon's Trainium custom AI training silicon represents the most underestimated AI investment opportunity in the market, suggesting the street is systematically mispricing this capability within AMZN shares. His core reasoning is that Trainium reduces AWS's dependence on Nvidia GPUs, which carries significant margin and competitive implications: as AWS deploys more proprietary silicon, it captures more of the value chain internally rather than paying Nvidia's premium pricing. The investment implication is that AWS margin expansion and competitive positioning in AI cloud services are not yet reflected in AMZN's valuation, making it a contrarian long. Baker's use of superlative framing ('most underestimated AI play in the market') signals this is a prepared, high-conviction differentiated view rather than a passing observation. No specific revenue figures or timeframes were cited, but the medium-term horizon implies Baker expects Trainium's impact to become visible in AWS financials within the next one to three years.

source: Sohn NY 2026 — Tech investing fireside
summary: AI frontier model pricing is shifting from flat-rate subscriptions to usage-based with overage, which is wildly bullish for AI revenue and token pricing
summary_extended: Baker argues that the AI frontier model industry is undergoing a fundamental pricing shift analogous to the cellular industry's transition from flat-rate minute plans to per-minute overage pricing — a shift he characterizes as 'wildly bullish' for AI revenue and token economics. He contends that usage-based pricing with overages, now being adopted by leading frontier model providers, will dramatically expand monetization per user and unlock massive revenue growth. A key structural observation is that only 10 basis points (0.1%) of the global population currently uses frontier AI models at the intensity they should, and scaling that to even 5% of global population would create compute demand that is, in his words, 'unimaginable.' Baker endorses a near-term combined revenue target of $200B+ for OpenAI and Anthropic, implying these two private companies alone could rival the largest software businesses ever built. The investment implication is bullish on the entire AI compute and infrastructure stack, but the direct revenue beneficiaries — OpenAI and Anthropic — are private and not US-listed equities, leaving no directly actionable public ticker from this specific thesis.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: AI has net destroyed trillions of dollars of value at the application layer, and value creation is concentrated in companies with the highest ratio of utilized GPUs per human
summary_extended: Baker argues that AI has been a net destroyer of value at the application layer, having wiped out trillions of dollars in market capitalization even when accounting for the success stories like Cursor and Cognition. His reasoning is structural: the companies that are actually creating economic value in the AI era are those with the highest ratio of utilized GPUs per human employee, meaning capital-efficient, AI-native businesses rather than traditional software companies layering AI onto legacy architectures. The implication is deeply bearish for incumbent SaaS companies, which face disruption from leaner AI-native competitors that can deliver similar or superior functionality with far fewer human resources. Baker frames this as an empirical observation investors must internalize, not a speculative view — the value destruction has already occurred and the structural dynamic is already in motion. Investors should be cautious of legacy application-layer software companies and skeptical of the assumption that AI is uniformly additive to enterprise software valuations.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: Astera Labs is a high-conviction long that has been miscategorized as a 'copper loser' in market baskets, when it is structurally a beneficiary of both copper and optical connectivity
summary_extended: Baker argues that Astera Labs (ALAB) has been systematically miscategorized in market 'copper loser' baskets, creating a significant mispricing opportunity. His structural argument is that Astera's primary product is a switch, and switches by definition connect to accelerators using both copper and optical connectivity — meaning Astera cannot be a copper loser regardless of how the market has labeled it. Baker has high conviction rooted in a long relationship with the company dating back to a Series C investment, giving him deep fundamental knowledge of the business. The miscategorization in leveraged short baskets has artificially depressed the stock, and Baker frames this as one of the largest opportunities he sees, contrasting ALAB with low-quality names that have already run 10x in 3-6 months and are likely to revert. The investment implication is a long position in ALAB, which benefits structurally from both copper and optical connectivity adoption in AI datacenter infrastructure.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: Anthropic's ARR growth is the most extraordinary revenue ramp in the history of capitalism, adding the combined ARR of Palantir, Snowflake, and Databricks in one month
summary_extended: Baker argues that Anthropic's revenue ramp is the most extraordinary in the history of capitalism, citing that the company added $11B of ARR in a single month — equivalent to the combined ARR of Palantir, Snowflake, and Databricks, three of the highest-profile SaaS companies of the last decade, each built over roughly ten years. He uses this data point as primary evidence that frontier AI demand is real, accelerating, and not slowing, which justified his decision to lean into AI positions rather than reduce exposure during the March/April 2026 market drawdown. The investment implication is that the AI model layer is experiencing demand that dwarfs prior software cycles, and that investors who sold during the drawdown were making a mistake. Baker's framing also implies that traditional SaaS companies like Palantir and Snowflake face existential disruption risk as AI-native platforms capture revenue at a pace legacy SaaS cannot match.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: Cross-sectional AI valuations are deeply inconsistent — semi-cap equipment at 40x annualized earnings vs. DRAM at mid-single digit P/E cannot both be correct
summary_extended: Baker argues that current cross-sectional valuations across the semiconductor supply chain are deeply inconsistent and cannot simultaneously be correct. Specifically, he notes that semi-cap equipment stocks are trading at approximately 40x annualized earnings while DRAM stocks trade at mid-single-digit P/Es — a stark contrast to the prior cycle peak where semi-cap traded at ~5x and DRAM at ~12x, meaning the historical relationship has essentially inverted. He contends this gap is unjustifiable even after accounting for improved semi-cap business model quality and uncertainty around HBM demand, and that one or both asset classes must reprice. He further highlights a second internal inconsistency: Nvidia's valuation appears cheap relative to its own history, while GE Vernova's valuation implies power demand growth so extreme it would require an unfathomable loss of Nvidia GPU market share — these two valuations cannot coexist. The investment implication is to be long DRAM (particularly Micron as the US-listed pure play) and skeptical of semi-cap equipment multiples, while the Nvidia/GEV inconsistency suggests either GEV is overvalued or NVDA is undervalued.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: The disaggregation of prefill and inference extends GPU useful lives to 10-15 years, which is structurally positive for private credit GPU financing and lowers the cost of the AI buildout
summary_extended: Baker argues that the architectural disaggregation of AI inference into separate prefill and decode stages structurally extends the useful life of older GPUs (Hopper, Ampere-generation) to 10-15 years, far beyond the 1-4 year useful life assumed by AI skeptics and embedded in current financing structures. His reasoning is that older chips can handle the compute-intensive prefill workload while newer chips handle latency-sensitive decode, meaning older hardware retains genuine economic utility rather than becoming obsolete. The investment implication is that GPU-backed private credit financing rates, currently in the low-7% range (citing CoreWeave as the benchmark), could compress to 5-6% as lenders recognize longer asset lives, materially reducing the total cost of the AI infrastructure buildout. Baker frames this as a structural positive for GPU financing vehicles and cloud/hyperscale operators who lease GPU capacity, and suggests it could offset losses private credit lenders face from deteriorating SaaS loan books. He cites no specific timeframe for the rate compression but treats the useful-life extension as an already-emerging structural reality rather than a speculative future outcome.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: Economic returns at the AI model layer are overwhelmingly concentrated at the frontier, which is surprising and may continue — this is the most important question for AI investors
summary_extended: Baker argues that economic returns at the AI model layer are overwhelmingly concentrated at the frontier — meaning the most capable, highest-cost models from providers like Anthropic, OpenAI, and xAI — rather than being distributed across cheaper or open-source alternatives, which he finds surprising given how rapidly older models have commoditized. His evidence includes his own direct experience observing model quality degradation when switching away from frontier models, suggesting enterprises and developers consistently return to paying premium prices for top-tier outputs. He frames this concentration as the single most important structural question for AI investors, because if frontier economics persist, the companies building and selling frontier tokens will capture disproportionate value even as the broader model market becomes crowded. He identifies two related open questions that compound this thesis: whether the 'bitter lesson' (that scale always wins) will be violated at the ASI threshold, and when continual/online learning will arrive to potentially reshape the competitive landscape. The investment implication is that investors should focus on who controls frontier model access, but all three named beneficiaries — Anthropic, OpenAI, and xAI — are private, leaving no directly actionable US-listed equity plays from this thesis alone.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: Google has lost its per-cost-token leadership due to conservative TPU v8 design decisions, shifting the Pareto frontier to Anthropic, OpenAI, and xAI
summary_extended: Baker argues that Google has lost its dominant position on the AI Pareto frontier (intelligence vs. cost-per-token) over the past nine months, with Anthropic, OpenAI, and xAI's Grok now leading while Google's Gemini 3.1 merely 'hangs on' to the frontier — and likely only through subsidization driven by competitive pride. The root cause, Baker contends, was a deliberate and conservative design decision on TPU v8 intended to reduce Google's dependence on Broadcom (for networking) and Nvidia (for GPUs), which resulted in a chip that underperforms on the cost-efficiency curve relative to competitors using more aggressive third-party silicon. The investment implication is negative for Google's AI monetization and cloud competitiveness, as losing cost-per-token leadership means Google cannot attract or retain the most cost-sensitive AI workloads. Baker frames this as a structural, self-inflicted competitive disadvantage rather than a temporary gap, suggesting the shift in frontier leadership may persist until TPU v9 or a course correction in chip design philosophy. All three frontier leaders — Anthropic, OpenAI, and xAI — remain private, limiting direct investable expression of the beneficiary side of this thesis.

He introduces a proprietary valuation framework called 'unconstrained run rate revenue' (URR), which estimates what a company's ARR would be if compute supply were not a bottleneck; by his estimate, Anthropic's URR is $100-200B, making its ~$900B valuation appear closer to 5x URR rather than an extreme multiple on current revenue. Baker's reasoning centers on Anthropic's lower cost per token and superior inference economics relative to OpenAI, suggesting structural advantages in the model layer that compound as compute costs decline. The investment implication is primarily relevant to private market investors or those tracking the frontier AI competitive landscape, as both Anthropic and OpenAI remain private; no directly tradeable public equity is the clear subject of this thesis.

Satya Nadella is now pivoting to use that compute internally to improve Copilot and train Microsoft's own frontier models rather than selling capacity to OpenAI, which Baker views as the right long-term strategic bet but a risky one given his skepticism about Microsoft's ability to compete at the frontier model level. Baker provides a specific counterfactual: if Microsoft had simply continued serving OpenAI and Anthropic with its GPU capacity as a pure infrastructure play, the stock would be worth $800 today, implying the market is not fully pricing in the execution risk of the internal AI product strategy. The investment implication is nuanced — Baker gives Satya credit for strategic courage but is not unambiguously bullish, flagging team capability as the key risk to watch on the internal model training ambition.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: Capitalism will solve the power/watts shortage for AI data centers by 2027-2028, with orbital compute as the longer-term solution
summary_extended: Baker argues that the primary bottleneck for AI data center buildout has shifted from chips or energy supply to zoning and regulatory approvals, and that capitalism will resolve the watts shortage by 2027-2028 as turbine manufacturing capacity ramps and alternative energy sources come online. He bases this on direct time spent at SpaceX's Starbase facility with engineers who are highly confident in solving the technical challenges of orbital compute — racks in space cooled and connected by lasers — as the longer-term structural solution to power constraints. The investment implication is that power generation and grid infrastructure companies stand to benefit in the medium term as data center buildout accelerates once regulatory bottlenecks clear, while SpaceX (private) is the primary vehicle for the orbital compute thesis. Baker cites a specific timeline of 2027-2028 for the watts shortage to begin alleviating, suggesting near-term power scarcity remains a tailwind for existing clean power producers with contracted capacity.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: Companies in the power production and cooling ecosystem that are massively ramping capacity face risk from orbital compute becoming real just as that capacity hits
summary_extended: Baker warns that companies in the terrestrial power production and cooling ecosystem that are currently ramping significant new capacity face a specific timing risk: orbital compute could become understood and priced by the market precisely as that new capacity comes online, potentially stranding or devaluing those investments. His reasoning is that if orbital compute eliminates or dramatically reduces the need for earth-based data center power and cooling infrastructure, the massive capital expenditures being made today by power and cooling companies could become impaired assets. The investment implication is a cautionary one — Baker is not explicitly shorting these names but urges investors in power/cooling infrastructure plays to think carefully about whether the timeline of their capacity additions aligns dangerously with the timeline of orbital compute becoming a credible market narrative. He stops short of naming specific companies, framing this as a structural risk warning rather than a specific sell recommendation, and the thesis is explicitly contrarian relative to the consensus bullish view on AI power infrastructure buildout.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: Orbital compute (racks in space connected by lasers) is a real and near-term solution to terrestrial power/cooling constraints, not science fiction
summary_extended: Baker argues that orbital compute — GPU racks deployed in space and interconnected via laser inter-satellite links — is a genuine near-term solution to terrestrial data center power and cooling constraints, not a speculative science fiction concept. He reframes the idea away from Pentagon-sized floating structures toward something concrete and already partially validated: a Blackwell rack weighs 3,000 lbs and stands 8 feet tall, making it physically compatible with heavy-lift launch vehicles, and SpaceX already operates 20kW Starlink satellites with high engineering confidence in scaling to 100-120kW per satellite. The laser inter-satellite link infrastructure needed to stitch orbital racks into a virtual data center already exists on every Starlink satellite, meaning the connectivity layer is not a future problem. Baker sees inference workloads — not training — as the natural fit for orbital compute, leaving terrestrial data centers intact for training, so this is not a bearish thesis on ground-based infrastructure within his investment horizon. The investment implication is that orbital compute is a structural shift worth pricing in now, with SpaceX (private) as the primary enabler, though no liquid US-listed equity is an unambiguous direct beneficiary based on the specifics he cited.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: TerraFab (SpaceX/Tesla joint venture with Intel partnership) will succeed in building a leading US semiconductor fab, potentially disrupting the TSMC/Intel/Samsung dynamic
summary_extended: Baker argues that TerraFab — a reported SpaceX/Tesla joint venture partnered with Intel — has a credible path to becoming a leading US semiconductor fab, potentially disrupting the current TSMC/Samsung/Intel hierarchy. His core reasoning draws on a structural analogy: just as TSMC rose to dominance partly because wafer fab equipment companies (ASML, KLA, Lam, Applied Materials) prioritized it with their best engineering teams, TerraFab will receive similar A-team support because those same equipment vendors want to avoid a TSMC monopsony and will back a credible challenger. Baker adds that Elon Musk's global reputation will attract elite semiconductor engineering talent, and that he will build cultural infrastructure — Taiwan towns, Japan towns, Korea towns — to recruit diaspora engineers who built TSMC and Samsung. Baker believes Elon will compress traditional fab development timelines significantly, making TerraFab a long-term structural threat to incumbent fabs and a long-term beneficiary thesis for the semi-cap equipment suppliers who will be deeply embedded in its buildout.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: TSMC's capacity discipline is the single most important variable in preventing an AI infrastructure bubble
summary_extended: Baker argues that TSMC's capacity discipline is the single most important variable determining whether AI infrastructure investment tips into an overbuild bubble. His core claim is that if TSMC were to fulfill Jensen Huang's full demand ambitions, Nvidia could theoretically sell $2–3 trillion worth of GPUs in 2026–27, a pace that would almost certainly result in massive overcapacity and a bubble. Baker identifies a 'Goldilocks zone' for TSMC: expand capacity enough to prevent Intel or Samsung from capturing more than roughly 30% market share as a second-source foundry (which would undermine TSMC's pricing discipline), but constrain supply enough to prevent runaway GPU shipments. He concludes that TSMC's capacity announcements and expansion decisions are the single best leading indicator an investor can monitor to assess AI infrastructure bubble risk, making TSM the central analytical lens for this thesis.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: Amazon is in a strong position due to Trainium, real P&L efficiencies from robotics in retail over the next 18 months, and underappreciated Nova models
summary_extended: Baker argues that Amazon is in a stronger competitive position than the market appreciates across three distinct vectors: its proprietary Trainium AI chip, robotics-driven retail efficiency, and undervalued Nova foundation models. On the chip side, Baker highlights Trainium 3's technical architecture — specifically its switch scale-up network — as enabling economical Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model inference, positioning Amazon to reduce dependence on NVIDIA and lower AI infrastructure costs. Baker expects robotics deployments in Amazon's retail and fulfillment operations to produce visible, measurable P&L improvements within 18 months, a catalyst he believes is underappreciated by the market. He also contends that Amazon's Nova models are better than their public reputation suggests, though he stops short of claiming they rival top-tier frontier models. The combined implication is that AMZN is a buy with multiple underappreciated near-to-medium-term earnings catalysts.

source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: The shift from all-you-can-eat to usage-based pricing at AI frontier labs is a major bullish catalyst for OpenAI and Anthropic revenue, likely driving both well above $200B ARR in 2026
summary_extended: Baker argues that the AI frontier lab industry is undergoing a structural pricing shift from flat-rate, all-you-can-eat subscriptions to usage-based, pay-per-token enterprise plans — a transition he analogizes to the cellular industry, where fixed plus usage-based pricing drove massive revenue growth while unlimited plans suppressed it. He contends that users who were previously rate-limited on $250–$300/month consumer plans will migrate to enterprise usage-based contracts, unlocking enormous incremental revenue that was previously capped. Baker makes a specific and high-conviction prediction that both OpenAI and Anthropic will 'well exceed $200B ARR' in 2026 as a direct result of this pricing dynamic. The investment implication is primarily relevant to private market investors, as both named beneficiaries are privately held; however, the thesis implies that the AI model layer is monetizing far faster than consensus expects, which could have read-through implications for public companies exposed to AI infrastructure demand.

source: All-In Podcast — E274: SpaceX $2T case, Nvidia shock selloff
summary: AI inference revenue is approaching $200-400B ARR across major LLM providers by end of 2026, validating ROI on GPU spend
summary_extended: Baker argues that AI inference is already generating massive, real revenue — estimating that OpenAI and Anthropic alone are running at approximately $100B ARR with roughly 80% gross margins on inference workloads. Adding Gemini (Google), Cursor, xAI, and open-source inference deployments, he projects total LLM inference ARR of $200-400B by end of 2026 at high gross margins. He also notes that GPU returns from improved recommender systems at Meta, Google, and Amazon provide additional ROI validation beyond pure LLM inference revenue.

He argues this is 'a really important fact for the whole AI narrative' because it demonstrates that AI companies can generate real returns rather than relying on circular funding from hyperscalers and venture capital. The implication is that AI monetization is now proven at scale, validating the investment thesis for the entire AI ecosystem rather than just individual companies. Baker returns to this point multiple times in the conversation, suggesting he views it as a structural inflection point — the transition from speculative AI spending to self-sustaining AI businesses — that should increase investor confidence across the sector. source: All-In Podcast — E274: SpaceX $2T case, Nvidia shock selloff
summary: Selling depreciated Nvidia GPUs to China reduces the probability of China developing a more power-hungry alternative AI ecosystem and is stabilizing for US AI leadership
summary_extended: Baker argues that selling older, depreciated Nvidia GPUs to China is the highest-probability path to preserving US AI leadership and maintaining American control over the global AI ecosystem. His reasoning is that if China is denied access to these chips, they are incentivized to develop their own alternative AI hardware stack — one that would be more power-hungry and would adopt optical scale-up fabrics earlier, ultimately reducing US strategic leverage. By contrast, keeping China dependent on Nvidia's architecture perpetuates US ecosystem dominance and slows Chinese indigenous chip development. Baker frames this as a contrarian view against export control hawks, suggesting the conventional wisdom of blanket restrictions may backfire by accelerating Chinese self-sufficiency. The investment implication is modestly positive for Nvidia, as a policy shift toward allowing depreciated GPU sales to China would open an incremental revenue channel that is currently restricted.

source: All-In Podcast — E274: SpaceX $2T case, Nvidia shock selloff
summary: Selling depreciated Nvidia GPUs to China reduces the probability of China developing a more power-hungry alternative AI ecosystem and is stabilizing for US AI leadership
summary_extended: Baker argues that selling older, depreciated Nvidia GPUs to China is the highest-probability path to preserving US AI leadership and maintaining American control over the global AI ecosystem. His reasoning is that if China is denied access to these chips, they are incentivized to develop their own alternative AI hardware stack — one that would be more power-hungry and would adopt optical scale-up fabrics earlier, ultimately reducing US strategic leverage. By contrast, keeping China dependent on Nvidia's architecture perpetuates US ecosystem dominance and slows Chinese indigenous chip development. Baker frames this as a contrarian view against export control hawks, suggesting the conventional wisdom of blanket restrictions may backfire by accelerating Chinese self-sufficiency. The investment implication is modestly positive for Nvidia, as a policy shift toward allowing depreciated GPU sales to China would open an incremental revenue channel that is currently restricted.

source: All-In Podcast — E274: SpaceX $2T case, Nvidia shock selloff
summary: The Strait of Hormuz closure is relatively good for America and Trump's policy goals due to US energy self-sufficiency, and is a forcing function for reindustrialization
summary_extended: Baker argues that a Strait of Hormuz closure is net negative for the global economy but relatively advantageous for the United States compared to its geopolitical rivals. His reasoning rests on US energy self-sufficiency: the US runs on domestic natural gas for electricity generation and is the world's largest oil and gas exporter, meaning it is insulated from the supply shock that would devastate energy-import-dependent Europe and Asia. He cites specific data to support the asymmetry: domestic natural gas futures (NG1) are down year-to-date while LNG prices for the rest of the world have surged 100-200%, illustrating the divergence in energy cost burdens. The investment implication is that every day the Strait remains closed accelerates US reindustrialization relative to competitors, as higher energy costs abroad erode the manufacturing competitiveness of Europe and Asia. Baker frames this as a structural, if uncomfortable, tailwind for Trump's policy goal of bringing industrial production back to the United States.

source: All-In Podcast — E274: SpaceX $2T case, Nvidia shock selloff
summary: Orbital/space-based compute is real and will hit SpaceX's bottom line in second half of 2028 to first half of 2030
summary_extended: Baker argues that orbital/space-based compute will become a commercial reality on SpaceX's P&L between H2 2028 and H1 2030, representing a significant new revenue stream for the company. He supports this view by pointing to a working H100 GPU already operating in space today — noting that Andrej Karpathy has trained a model on it — as evidence that the technical barriers are being overcome faster than the market appreciates. A key underappreciated competitive advantage Baker highlights is SpaceX's ability to use non-radiation-hardened commercial chips in space because their hardware happens to pass radiation testing, dramatically lowering the cost and complexity of deploying compute in orbit versus traditional space-grade hardware. The primary investment implication is bullish on SpaceX (private), with no clear publicly-traded direct beneficiary that Baker specifies — NVDA is mentioned only as the manufacturer of the H100 already in orbit, not as a structural beneficiary of this thesis.

source: All-In Podcast — E274: SpaceX $2T case, Nvidia shock selloff
summary: SpaceX's Elon Web Services (EWS) data center business can grow dramatically faster than previously contemplated, with the Anthropic $15B/year contract as proof of concept
summary_extended: Baker argues that SpaceX's Elon Web Services (EWS) data center business has a dramatically larger growth trajectory than previously understood, with Anthropic's $15B/year compute contract ($1.25B/month for Colossus 1 and parts of Colossus 2) serving as the proof-of-concept anchor tenant that de-risks rapid capacity expansion. He highlights SpaceX's extraordinary construction velocity — compressing build times from 122 days to 91 days to 66 days per data center — and lower costs relative to hyperscalers, arguing these operational advantages make SpaceX the fastest GPU deployment platform in the industry. Baker notes that Jensen Huang allocates GPUs to whoever can plug them in fastest, meaning SpaceX's construction speed creates a self-reinforcing advantage in securing Nvidia GPU supply. The investment implication is that SpaceX (private) is the primary beneficiary, and the thesis does not cleanly translate to any publicly listed equity; NVDA is a loose downstream beneficiary only insofar as faster SpaceX buildout accelerates GPU consumption, but this is not a thesis specifically about Nvidia. Baker frames this as a medium-term structural re-rating of EWS's addressable market, not a near-term trade on any listed stock.

source: All-In Podcast — E274: SpaceX $2T case, Nvidia shock selloff
summary: xAI (Grok) is now on the Pareto frontier of frontier models and the release of Grok Build (harness/runtime) is a critical missing piece that makes the model dramatically more useful
summary_extended: Baker argues that xAI's Grok 4.3 (a 500B parameter model) has reached the Pareto frontier of frontier AI models, placing it alongside Google Gemini 3.1 Pro, OpenAI, and Anthropic's offerings in terms of capability. Previously, Grok lacked a harness — a runtime/environment that manages state and memory in agentic workflows — which Baker identifies as a critical gap relative to competitors like Claude Code (Anthropic) and Codex (OpenAI). The release of Grok Build fills this gap, and Baker contends that the harness is 'essentially as important as the model' in an agentic world, meaning raw model quality alone is insufficient for real-world deployment. Baker further argues that harnesses must be co-developed with the underlying model, suggesting that xAI's integrated approach positions Grok Build as a structurally competitive product rather than an afterthought. The investment implication is that xAI is now a credible competitor in the agentic AI space, though xAI is not publicly listed, limiting direct equity expression of this thesis.

source: Limitless (Bankless) — AI supercycle; Unity as world-model builder; chips/memory/power
summary: AI is in a supercycle, not a bubble — constrained by physical supply of watts and wafers, not debt-fueled speculation
summary_extended: Baker argues that AI is in a structural supercycle rather than a speculative bubble, drawing a sharp contrast with the dot-com era by pointing out that hyperscalers — Google, Microsoft, Amazon, and Meta — are funding their AI buildouts entirely from free cash flow rather than debt or equity issuance. He cites OpenAI and Anthropic alone being on track for $200B in ARR as evidence of genuine, rapidly scaling demand, not speculative hype. Critically, he argues that physical supply constraints — specifically TSMC wafer capacity and global energy availability — act as a natural dampener against oversupply, making the classic bubble mechanism (cheap capital enabling reckless overbuilding) structurally impossible in this cycle. The investment implication is to remain long the AI infrastructure stack, as constrained supply meeting surging demand is a durable, multi-year earnings tailwind for the companies enabling that buildout. Baker frames this as his core, repeated thesis around which his fund is structured.
tickers_direct: GOOGL, MSFT, AMZN, META, TSM, NVDA

source: Limitless (Bankless) — AI supercycle; Unity as world-model builder; chips/memory/power
summary: Astera Labs is a critical bottleneck-solver for AI cluster connectivity and a top portfolio position
summary_extended: Baker argues that as AI clusters scale to hundreds of thousands of chips, the primary bottleneck shifts away from raw GPU compute toward data transfer and memory access — the 'plumbing' layer that connects chips within a cluster. Astera Labs (ALAB) directly addresses this connectivity bottleneck by providing the interconnect and memory interface solutions that enable large-scale AI infrastructure to function efficiently. Baker has made ALAB his second-largest portfolio position at approximately 7.4% of the fund, reflecting high conviction that this connectivity layer is a critical and underappreciated constraint on AI scaling. He draws an analogy to an early Cerebras thesis, suggesting he views ALAB as a similarly asymmetric opportunity in a structural bottleneck that the market may not yet fully appreciate. The investment implication is a concentrated long position in ALAB as the dominant pure-play on AI cluster connectivity infrastructure.

source: Limitless (Bankless) — AI supercycle; Unity as world-model builder; chips/memory/power
summary: SK Hynix's dominant HBM position gives it extraordinary pricing power, with ~70% operating margins
summary_extended: Baker argues that SK Hynix has achieved an extraordinary and historically unprecedented competitive position as the dominant HBM supplier to Nvidia, giving it near-monopolistic pricing power in the most critical segment of the AI memory market. He cites specific evidence: Google and Microsoft have reportedly approached SK Hynix with $50-100 billion offers to lock in future HBM supply, yet SK Hynix is reportedly declining guaranteed supply deals in favor of simply raising prices — a posture only possible when demand vastly exceeds supply. Baker highlights SK Hynix's ~70% operating margins as validation of this thesis, describing such margins as essentially unheard of in the semiconductor industry, which historically operates at far thinner margins. The investment implication is that the HBM memory supply constraint is real and durable, and that SK Hynix is the primary beneficiary — though as a Korean-listed stock (000660.KS), US investors seeking exposure to this thesis must look to US-listed proxies such as Micron (MU), the only US-listed HBM producer with meaningful scale.

theme: Edge AI as bear case for cloud
source: Limitless (Bankless) — AI supercycle; Unity as world-model builder; chips/memory/power
summary: Verticalized small language models (SLMs) running on-device are a major coming wave, with Apple as a key enabler
summary_extended: Baker argues that verticalized small language models (SLMs) running on-device represent one of four major investment pillars in the coming AI wave, driven by enterprise and consumer demand for data privacy in sensitive verticals such as medical and financial services. He contends that locally run models avoid the privacy risks of cloud-based inference, making on-device deployment a structural necessity rather than a niche use case. Apple is identified as the primary hardware platform enabling this trend, given its dominant installed base of high-end consumer devices capable of running local inference workloads. However, Baker explicitly does not hold Apple as an investment expression of this thesis, suggesting he has not found a clean way to play the SLM-on-device theme through publicly traded equities. The investment implication is to watch for pure-play SLM developers or enterprise software companies building verticalized models, though no specific investable names beyond Apple are identified in this thesis.

source: Limitless (Bankless) — AI supercycle; Unity as world-model builder; chips/memory/power
summary: Orbital/space compute is an emerging frontier for AI infrastructure, with SpaceX as the critical enabler
summary_extended: Baker argues that orbital and space-based compute represents an emerging frontier for AI infrastructure, driven by terrestrial constraints on energy availability and land use that are increasingly limiting data center buildout. He contends that SpaceX's Starship rocket is the essential enabler for this vision, as only Starship's payload economics make it feasible to deliver server racks and energy infrastructure to orbit at scale — without it, the entire concept is non-viable. Baker frames this as an early-stage, speculative but strategically important thesis, representing a forward-looking shift in his thinking about where AI compute infrastructure ultimately goes. Because SpaceX is private and no pure-play public orbital compute company exists, Baker himself acknowledges limited actionability in public markets, which the hosts note is reflected in his actual 13F holdings. No specific numbers or timeframes were cited, but the implication is that this is a multi-year, long-duration structural theme to monitor rather than act on immediately.

source: Limitless (Bankless) — AI supercycle; Unity as world-model builder; chips/memory/power
summary: TSMC supply constraint is the primary governor preventing the AI buildout from becoming a debt-fueled bubble
summary_extended: Baker argues that TSMC's manufacturing capacity is the single binding constraint preventing the AI infrastructure buildout from becoming an unsustainable, debt-fueled bubble. He contends that Nvidia could sell $2–3 trillion worth of GPUs annually if TSMC could supply the necessary silicon, meaning demand is effectively uncapped but supply is rationed by TSMC's inability to scale rapidly. This supply constraint imposes a natural, sustainable pace on AI capital expenditure, which Baker views as a structural safeguard rather than a problem. ASML's monopoly on EUV lithography machines — with a five-year backlog, ~$400M per unit, and a single production team — further entrenches TSMC's bottleneck and makes any rapid capacity expansion by competitors essentially impossible in the near term. Baker dismisses Samsung as a credible near-term threat to TSMC's leading-edge dominance, reinforcing the view that this constraint is durable and investable.

source: Limitless (Bankless) — AI supercycle; Unity as world-model builder; chips/memory/power
summary: Unity Software is a world-model builder for robotics and AGI training, making it a sleeper AI infrastructure play
summary_extended: Baker argues that Unity Software (U) is a sleeper AI infrastructure play because its 3D game engine encodes deep, physics-accurate understanding of the real world — including lighting, texture, and physical dynamics — making it uniquely suited to generate synthetic training environments for humanoid robots and embodied AI systems. He contends that as world models become the dominant training paradigm for next-generation AI (particularly for robotics and AGI), Unity's engine becomes critical infrastructure for producing the synthetic data needed to train these systems at scale. Baker holds Unity as his third-largest position, signaling high conviction despite the thesis being forward-looking and speculative on the timeline for world-model adoption. The investment implication is that Unity is being mispriced by the market as a struggling game engine company when it should be valued as foundational AI training infrastructure, with the payoff contingent on humanoid robotics and embodied AI scaling meaningfully over the long term.

source: All-In Podcast — Liquidity Summit: secondary markets eating the IPO
summary: Current technology valuations (public and private) are fully valued to stretched — not a bubble like 1999/2000, but more analogous to 2021, with risk of 10-20% public market correction translating to 30-40% drawdowns in high-beta names.
summary_extended: Baker and Gerstner jointly assert that technology valuations — both public and private — are fully valued to stretched following parabolic moves, drawing an explicit analogy to 2021 rather than the more extreme 1999/2000 bubble. Their reasoning is that the magnitude of recent gains has compressed future return potential and elevated downside risk, with a normal semiconductor-index correction of 10-20% capable of producing 30-40% drawdowns in high-beta names. The investment implication is defensive: both managers are actively selling into secondary market strength to return DPI (distributions to paid-in capital) to LPs, signaling a deliberate de-risking posture rather than a bullish hold. They stop short of calling a definitive top but are explicit that they are not buyers at current levels, describing valuations as 'extraordinary' — coded language for stretched. No specific single company is the subject; the thesis is a broad macro-valuation warning on the technology sector as a whole.

source: All-In Podcast — Liquidity Summit: secondary markets eating the IPO
summary: Arista Networks and Drivetrain (Driveats/Drivets) are high-conviction positions in the AI networking infrastructure supercycle thesis — reinventing networking for disaggregated AI inference workloads.
summary_extended: Baker argues that the shift toward disaggregated AI inference — where pre-fill and decode operations run on different, heterogeneous chips — requires a fundamental reinvention of data center networking, creating a multi-year infrastructure supercycle. He identifies Arista Networks (ANET) and a private company he refers to as 'Drivets' (likely a private networking startup) as two names he has been publicly involved in that are approaching this networking reinvention in a differentiated way. The investment implication is that traditional networking architectures are insufficient for orchestrating heterogeneous silicon at scale, and companies building purpose-built AI networking infrastructure stand to benefit enormously. Baker is described by co-host Gerstner as one of the earliest investors to frame the infra networking supercycle thesis, lending high conviction to the view. The thesis is long-term in nature and focused specifically on the networking layer of AI infrastructure, not compute or memory.

source: All-In Podcast — Liquidity Summit: secondary markets eating the IPO
summary: Zipline is a high-conviction private investment in autonomous drone delivery — a sector that had a false start but now works, with a path to dramatically lower delivery costs and massive consumption uplift.
summary_extended: Baker argues that Zipline represents a high-conviction private investment opportunity in autonomous drone delivery, a sector he believes has now proven itself after an earlier false start. The company spent seven years delivering medicines autonomously in Africa, accumulating irreplaceable real-world flight data and demonstrably cutting maternal mortality by 90-95% in some regions — evidence Baker cites as proof the technology genuinely works at scale. Zipline's key technical differentiator is a tether-drop delivery system that eliminates the need for landing, enabling urban and suburban deployment in the US market. Baker frames the economic model as 'Uber 2.0': reducing per-delivery costs from roughly $15 today to $5 and eventually ~$2, which he believes will trigger a massive consumption uplift analogous to how ride-sharing expanded transportation demand. As a private company, there is no directly tradeable US-listed equity, and Baker's investment is through Atreides' private holdings.

source: All-In Podcast — Liquidity Summit: secondary markets eating the IPO
summary: Secondary markets for late-stage private companies have become a structural, permanent feature of the capital markets ecosystem, now exceeding 2021 peak volumes and competing with IPOs and M&A as primary exit mechanisms.
summary_extended: Baker and fellow panelists argue that secondary markets for late-stage private companies have undergone a permanent structural transformation, not a cyclical uptick, with transaction volumes now running at double the 2021 peak. The evidence cited is striking: SpaceX alone accounts for 31% of all primary venture activity conducted via secondaries in 2025, and pricing has flipped from a discount (80 cents on the dollar) to a premium (106 cents on the dollar), reflecting robust institutional demand for pre-IPO exposure. The implication is that secondaries have become a co-equal exit mechanism alongside IPOs and M&A, fundamentally altering how late-stage private companies access liquidity and how investors gain exposure to them. Baker views this as a durable regime change in capital markets structure, suggesting that platforms and intermediaries facilitating secondary transactions stand to benefit over the long term. No specific public-market investment vehicle is identified as the primary beneficiary, though the thesis has implications for traditional IPO underwriters and exchange operators who may see reduced deal flow.

source: All-In Podcast — Liquidity Summit: secondary markets eating the IPO
summary: Venture firms without material exposure to trillion-dollar-plus private companies (SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI) face serious franchise risk and are engaging in undisciplined 'call option' behavior to compensate.
summary_extended: Baker argues that the VC industry is experiencing a structural bifurcation driven by the extreme concentration of value creation in a handful of mega-private companies — specifically SpaceX, Anthropic, and OpenAI — each valued at or above the trillion-dollar threshold. Firms that secured meaningful exposure to these winners are maintaining investment discipline and generating strong DPI, while those without exposure are watching their returns deteriorate from top-quintile to mediocre, creating existential franchise risk. In response to this franchise risk, the have-nots are engaging in undisciplined 'call option' behavior — making speculative bets on long-shot companies like 'Neolabs' in hopes of a lottery-ticket outcome that restores their standing. Baker presents this as a direct observation from current market behavior, not a prediction, suggesting the bifurcation is already underway. The investment implication is that the private VC landscape is increasingly winner-take-most, and LPs should scrutinize whether their VC managers have genuine exposure to the mega-privates or are compensating with low-quality speculation.

source: BG2 Pod — SpaceX IPO, AI capex, Cursor
summary: AI compute monetization rates per gigawatt are increasing, not deflating, contrary to consensus expectations entering 2026
summary_extended: Baker endorses a structural view that AI compute monetization rates per gigawatt are rising sharply, not deflating as consensus expected entering 2026, with Fox citing an increase from roughly $20B/GW at the start of the year to $30–40B/GW currently. The core reasoning is that token pricing is moving up, not down, because demand is outstripping supply — a dynamic Baker attributes in part to the extremely low current penetration of agentic AI use (less than 0.2% of people use AI agentically), meaning any meaningful scaling of that use case implies a sustained and prolonged compute shortage. Baker explicitly endorses owning asset-heavy businesses — hyperscalers, power infrastructure, and data center operators — in this inflationary token-pricing environment, arguing that the scarcity dynamic rewards capital-intensive incumbents. The investment implication is to be long the physical infrastructure stack (power, data centers, networking) that benefits from rising revenue-per-watt, rather than assuming commoditization of AI compute.

source: BG2 Pod — SpaceX IPO, AI capex, Cursor
summary: The 'finding the next bottleneck' trade in semiconductors is over; many bottleneck stocks have gone 'straight up a cliff' and need to rest or correct
summary_extended: Baker argues that the 'finding the next bottleneck' trade in semiconductors — where investors rotated into whichever component or subsystem was the next supply constraint in the AI infrastructure buildout — is now exhausted. He contends that many of the stocks that benefited from this rotation have gone 'straight up a cliff' rather than climbing gradually, leaving them technically extended and vulnerable to consolidation or correction. As evidence of his conviction, Baker has reduced his own semiconductor exposure from 'large' to 'medium-small,' and notes that even Nvidia and Broadcom, the two most prominent AI semiconductor names, have been laggards in the most recent leg of the rally — suggesting the easy money has already been made across the board. He also flags a seasonal near-term headwind: AI token consumption tends to slow in summer as college students (a major usage cohort) are less active, which could add incremental selling pressure. The investment implication is to reduce or avoid overweight positions in extended semiconductor bottleneck plays in the near term, with Baker himself having already acted on this view.

source: BG2 Pod — SpaceX IPO, AI capex, Cursor
summary: Coding is the fastest path to AGI/ASI and will continue to be the dominant high-value AI use case capturing frontier model revenue
summary_extended: Baker argues that coding is the dominant high-value AI use case and will remain so for the foreseeable future, citing Replit founder Amjad Masad's 'bitter lesson adjacent' insight that a model capable of coding can write code to do virtually anything, making coding the fastest path to AGI and ASI. The reasoning is structural: coding demand appears inexhaustible, enterprises will not tolerate second-tier code quality and therefore will not trade down from frontier models, and proprietary coding data accumulated by players like Cursor and xAI creates durable competitive moats. The investment implication is that frontier model providers capturing coding revenue — particularly those with coding-specific data advantages — are best positioned, and that coding will continue to concentrate AI revenue at the frontier rather than commoditizing to cheaper models. Baker corroborates this view with Anthropic revenue data and Cursor's growth trajectory, expressing high conviction with no hedging on the thesis.

source: BG2 Pod — SpaceX IPO, AI capex, Cursor
summary: Long-running agentic AI models represent a step-change in intelligence utilization that makes current benchmarks obsolete and dramatically increases compute demand
summary_extended: Baker argues that long-running agentic AI models represent a fundamental step-change in intelligence utilization that renders current benchmarks meaningless and dramatically expands compute demand. He references Noam Brown's insight that no one truly knows how capable current frontier models are because they have never been run continuously for extended periods — the analogy being that Einstein thinking about physics 24/7 without sleep, distraction, or aging would produce extraordinary output far beyond what episodic use suggests. This framing caused Baker to become significantly more bullish on compute than he already was, implying that the market is underestimating the long-run infrastructure buildout required to support persistent, multi-agent AI workloads. Fable 5/Mythos capabilities in multi-agent orchestration and long-running tasks are cited as early validation of this thesis, and Gerstner corroborates with Anthropic revenue inflection as evidence the demand signal is already materializing. The investment implication is to be long compute infrastructure — particularly GPU and datacenter capacity providers — as the true ceiling of model utilization has not yet been approached.

source: BG2 Pod — SpaceX IPO, AI capex, Cursor
summary: Frontier AI models continue to capture ~90%+ of economic value despite open-source models consuming the majority of tokens
summary_extended: Baker argues that the bear case for frontier AI models — that open-source would erode their economic value — has been 'decisively wrong,' with frontier models likely capturing 90%+ of AI revenue despite open-source models handling the majority of token volume by count. He presents these two facts as non-contradictory: token volume and economic value are decoupled, with frontier models commanding premium pricing that concentrates revenue even as open-source handles commodity workloads. As evidence, he cites Anthropic's strong revenue trajectory and the example of Harvey (an AI legal platform) which, despite routing some workloads to open-source models, still consumed significant Anthropic Opus tokens for high-value tasks. The investment implication is that frontier AI providers — primarily Anthropic and OpenAI, both private — are the primary beneficiaries, and the thesis validates continued aggressive capex spending by hyperscalers training and serving frontier models. Public market investors should watch GOOGL (Gemini) and META (open-source provider, somewhat contradicted by this thesis) as the closest listed proxies, though the direct beneficiaries remain private.

source: BG2 Pod — SpaceX IPO, AI capex, Cursor
summary: In a power-constrained world, tokens-per-watt advantage makes Nvidia superior to alternatives even at higher cost
summary_extended: Baker argues that in a power-constrained AI infrastructure environment, Nvidia's superior tokens-per-watt efficiency translates directly into higher revenue generation for datacenter operators, making Nvidia's premium pricing structurally justified rather than merely a performance luxury. His core reasoning is that when power (watts) is the binding constraint on compute capacity, the chip that produces more tokens per watt effectively produces more billable output per unit of scarce resource — meaning more revenue per rack. Baker explicitly endorses Jensen Huang's framing that 'more tokens per watt is literally revenue,' and argues that a hyperscaler or cloud operator choosing a cheaper but less efficient alternative saves on capex but sacrifices top-line revenue and potentially margin. The investment implication is that Nvidia's pricing power is durable and structurally anchored to the economics of power-constrained datacenters, not just benchmark superiority, and that competitors offering lower-cost alternatives face a fundamental revenue-generation disadvantage in this environment. No specific numerical targets or timeframes are cited, but Baker frames this as a medium-term structural dynamic tied to ongoing power scarcity in AI infrastructure build-outs.

theme: Open source AI bullish for hardware
source: BG2 Pod — SpaceX IPO, AI capex, Cursor
summary: Open-source AI success is bullish for compute/hardware providers, not bearish for the AI ecosystem
summary_extended: Baker argues that the rise of open-source AI models is structurally bullish for compute and hardware providers, not bearish for the AI ecosystem as commonly feared. His reasoning is that if frontier proprietary models face margin compression from open-source competition, AI spending does not disappear — it shifts toward compute infrastructure, since open-source models still require massive hardware to train and run at scale. The better open-source models perform, the more inference and training compute is demanded across a broader set of users and enterprises who would otherwise have paid for proprietary API access. This reframes the conventional open-source bear case (that it destroys AI monetization) into a compute bull case: commoditization of model intelligence accelerates hardware consumption. The investment implication is to be long compute and semiconductor infrastructure providers that supply the GPUs, networking, and accelerators powering this expanded workload.

source: BG2 Pod — SpaceX IPO, AI capex, Cursor
summary: SpaceX's terrestrial AI compute business is monetizing at dramatically higher rates than peers and is the most underappreciated near-term revenue driver
summary_extended: Baker argues that SpaceX's terrestrial AI compute business is dramatically underappreciated because its monetization rates far exceed what Wall Street models imply. Specifically, SpaceX signed Google at approximately $50B per gigawatt per year and Anthropic at $22-23B per gigawatt per year, compared to only ~$14B per gigawatt implied in sell-side bank models — representing a 60-250% premium to consensus assumptions. Baker notes that SpaceX went from zero presence as an AI hyperscaler to being the #4 player in roughly 30 days, surpassing Oracle and others, which he views as a structural inflection point in the company's revenue profile. The investment implication is that SpaceX's terrestrial compute business alone justifies significant valuation upside even before any optionality from orbital compute, making any future public listing a high-conviction opportunity. Since SpaceX remains private, the thesis has limited direct public-market expression, though GOOGL is a confirmed customer at the cited deal economics.

source: BG2 Pod — SpaceX IPO, AI capex, Cursor
summary: AI compute monetization rates per gigawatt are increasing, not deflating, contrary to consensus expectations entering 2026
summary_extended: Baker endorses a structural view that AI compute monetization rates per gigawatt are rising sharply, not deflating as consensus expected entering 2026, with Fox citing an increase from roughly $20B/GW at the start of the year to $30–40B/GW currently. The core reasoning is that token pricing is moving up, not down, because demand is outstripping supply — a dynamic Baker attributes in part to the extremely low current penetration of agentic AI use (less than 0.2% of people use AI agentically), meaning any meaningful scaling of that use case implies a sustained and prolonged compute shortage. Baker explicitly endorses owning asset-heavy businesses — hyperscalers, power infrastructure, and data center operators — in this inflationary token-pricing environment, arguing that the scarcity dynamic rewards capital-intensive incumbents. The investment implication is to be long the physical infrastructure stack (power, data centers, networking) that benefits from rising revenue-per-watt, rather than assuming commoditization of AI compute.

source: BG2 Pod — SpaceX IPO, AI capex, Cursor
summary: SpaceX is a must-own for institutional investors as the best single bet on both the AI and space future
summary_extended: Baker argues that SpaceX is a must-own, 'set it and forget it' holding for institutional investors, representing the single best bet on both the AI and space future simultaneously. He frames SpaceX as uniquely diversified across four high-conviction verticals: launch services, Starlink satellite internet, terrestrial AI compute infrastructure, and frontier model development — a combination no other company or entrepreneur can match. Baker's conviction is reinforced by his existing large shareholder position, and he uses unhedged superlative language ('must buy, must own') to convey that this is a core long-term holding rather than a tactical trade. The investment implication is straightforward: institutional investors without SpaceX exposure are underweight the most important private company in the world, and the anticipated IPO would be a generational buying opportunity. No specific price targets or timeframes were cited, but the thesis is explicitly long-term and structural.

source: BG2 Pod — SpaceX IPO, AI capex, Cursor
summary: Cursor acquisition dramatically advances XAI/SpaceX's frontier model capability and is the most underappreciated upside in the SpaceX IPO
summary_extended: Baker argues that the Cursor acquisition by xAI is the single most underappreciated element of the anticipated SpaceX IPO, and that its impact on frontier model capability is being systematically overlooked by the market. The core thesis is that Cursor possesses a proprietary dataset of coding tokens that exceeds what is available on the public internet, giving xAI a unique and non-replicable training advantage. Baker provides specific technical evidence: Composer 2.5, trained on a Kimi K2.5 base combined with Cursor data and reinforcement learning over just three weeks on Colossus 2, was already Pareto dominant; and Grok 4.3, a 1.5 trillion parameter model, is now incorporating Cursor data directly into pre-training rather than only RL fine-tuning, which is a qualitatively more powerful integration. The investment implication is that when SpaceX eventually IPOs, the xAI frontier model capability — supercharged by Cursor's proprietary data — represents a significant upside surprise that the market has not yet priced in, making it the most compelling hidden value driver in the anticipated offering.

source: CNBC Squawk on the Street — SpaceX public-debut day: orbital compute economics, compute shortage, IPO
summary: Terrestrial data centers will never be decommissioned — once built they retain value — but orbital compute may dominate new installations within 10-20 years
summary_extended: Baker argues that while terrestrial data centers will never be decommissioned and will retain their value, the overwhelming majority of net-new compute installed to serve planet Earth could be orbital within a 10-20 year timeframe, representing a fundamental structural shift in where incremental compute capacity is built. His reasoning is directional rather than near-term actionable — he uses hedged language ('I think,' 'there may be a day') and a wide time horizon, suggesting this is a long-term structural view about the economics of orbital versus terrestrial compute. The investment implication is a bifurcated future: existing terrestrial data center assets (REITs, operators) retain value as legacy infrastructure, while the incremental growth opportunity shifts toward orbital compute providers. Baker made these comments on SpaceX's public-debut day, implying SpaceX is the primary vehicle he has in mind for orbital compute, though no specific US-listed pure-play orbital compute equity exists today beyond SpaceX itself, which remains private.

source: CNBC Squawk on the Street — SpaceX public-debut day: orbital compute economics, compute shortage, IPO
summary: The global compute shortage is severe and worsening, supporting pricing power for all compute providers including neoclouds
summary_extended: Baker argues that the global compute shortage is severe and intensifying on a near-daily basis, making it one of the most pressing constraints in the AI infrastructure buildout. He emphasizes that while token prices and GPU rental rates fluctuate at the surface level, the underlying supply-demand imbalance is real and broadly recognized by industry insiders with direct visibility into capacity utilization. This structural shortage supports sustained pricing power and demand for any entity providing compute capacity, particularly neoclouds and GPU cloud providers. The implication is that terrestrial data center and GPU rental businesses remain attractive investments even as new compute modalities like orbital compute emerge as potential future competition.
override_note: All mentions. He named both CoreWeave and Nebius at Sohn NY 2026.

source: CNBC Squawk on the Street — SpaceX public-debut day: orbital compute economics, compute shortage, IPO
summary: Orbital compute will be structurally cheaper than terrestrial compute once Starship is reusable, making space the dominant long-term compute platform
summary_extended: Baker argues that orbital compute will become structurally cheaper than terrestrial compute once Starship achieves full reusability, presenting a detailed first-principles cost model to support this claim. A terrestrial gigawatt-scale data center costs approximately $60B, of which roughly $25B goes to power and cooling infrastructure and ~$10B to land and physical shell — neither of which is required in space — leaving ~$25-35B in GPU hardware. With reusable Starship, Baker estimates launch costs for a gigawatt of compute at ~$5B, yielding a total orbital cost of ~$30B versus ~$60B terrestrial, a roughly 50% structural cost advantage. The investment implication is that SpaceX (post-IPO) is the primary beneficiary as the sole vertically integrated operator of the launch vehicle enabling this cost structure, and Baker frames this as a long-term structural inevitability rather than a speculative bet. Open questions around on-orbit repair and maintenance remain, but Baker expresses high confidence that SpaceX will solve them.

source: CNBC Squawk on the Street — SpaceX public-debut day: orbital compute economics, compute shortage, IPO
summary: SpaceX's gigawatt terrestrial compute buildout is a near-term high-IRR business that should be valued like Amazon's early capex cycle
summary_extended: Baker argues that SpaceX's terrestrial compute buildout — specifically its Colossus 1 data center — represents a near-term, high-IRR capital deployment opportunity that the market should reward rather than penalize. He anchors this view on Altimeter's calculation of a 55% IRR on Colossus 1, arguing that when returns on invested capital are this high, heavy capex is value-creating, not value-destroying. Baker draws an explicit analogy to Amazon's early investment phase, suggesting that investors who understand the IRR math will be supportive of SpaceX's continued buildout. The key forward-looking variable, in his view, is how many gigawatts of compute capacity SpaceX can bring online over the next several years, which will determine the magnitude of value creation. As a large shareholder who participated in the IPO, Baker frames this as a core long position with a medium-term horizon tied to gigawatt-scale compute deployment.

source: CNBC Squawk on the Street — SpaceX public-debut day: orbital compute economics, compute shortage, IPO
summary: GPU scarcity gives SpaceX a structural advantage in securing Nvidia allocations because Jensen Huang prioritizes customers who will use GPUs immediately
summary_extended: Baker argues that Nvidia's Jensen Huang preferentially allocates scarce GPUs to customers who will deploy them immediately rather than queue them in a backlog, giving SpaceX a structural procurement advantage. The reasoning is that both GPU sellers (Nvidia) and power providers are incentivized to route constrained resources — chips and electricity — to whoever can put them to work fastest, and SpaceX's ability to bring gigawatts of power online rapidly makes it uniquely positioned to absorb large GPU allocations quickly. This creates a self-reinforcing advantage: SpaceX gets preferential GPU access because it can energize capacity fast, which in turn accelerates its orbital and terrestrial compute buildout ahead of competitors. Baker presents this as a logical inference consistent with known Nvidia allocation dynamics rather than confirmed fact, and the investment implication is that SpaceX's compute infrastructure position is stronger than it appears on paper — though SpaceX remains privately held, limiting direct public-market expression of this thesis.

source: CNBC Squawk on the Street — SpaceX public-debut day: orbital compute economics, compute shortage, IPO
summary: SpaceX's rapid entry into cloud computing positions it as a major hyperscaler within weeks of launch
summary_extended: Baker argues that SpaceX, within approximately one month of entering the cloud computing market, has by some measures become the fourth largest cloud provider globally, surpassing Oracle — a feat he presents as evidence of extraordinary commercial velocity. He frames this rapid ascent as a foundational premise for his SpaceX bull case, suggesting that if the company can achieve this scale in a single month, the trajectory over a full year could be genuinely transformative for the cloud and AI infrastructure landscape. Baker is a large shareholder and participated in the IPO, giving him direct financial exposure and high conviction in this view. The investment implication is a strong buy on SpaceX at IPO, with the cloud growth story serving as a key differentiator from traditional hyperscalers. No specific revenue figures or market share percentages were cited beyond the ordinal ranking claim.

source: TBPN — SpaceX IPO, token path, sovereign AI won't reach frontier
summary: The 'bottleneck trade' in AI supply chain is nearing its end; the next game is identifying companies with enduring franchise value
summary_extended: Baker argues that the 'bottleneck trade' in the AI supply chain — the strategy of buying companies that are temporary chokepoints in AI infrastructure buildout — is nearing its end as a viable investment thesis. His evidence is the recent Ajinomoto example, where the company declined to raise prices despite being a perceived bottleneck (in ABF substrate materials), disappointing investors who owned it purely as a supply-chain squeeze play. Baker contends the investment game is now shifting from 'what is the next bottleneck' to 'what has enduring franchise value,' implying that companies owned solely for scarcity pricing power will underperform as constraints ease. The implication is that investors should rotate away from pure bottleneck plays and toward companies with durable competitive moats in AI, though Baker does not name specific replacement holdings in this segment.

source: TBPN — SpaceX IPO, token path, sovereign AI won't reach frontier
summary: CDNs like Cloudflare are partially in the token path but currently deliver less than 1% (possibly <10bps) of global tokens consumed
summary_extended: Baker argues that CDN providers like Cloudflare are largely peripheral to the AI token delivery infrastructure today, estimating that CDNs collectively deliver less than 1% of global tokens consumed — and possibly as little as 10 basis points — with the vast majority of token delivery happening internally within hyperscaler infrastructure. His reasoning is that the real 'token path' is dominated by large cloud providers running inference at scale on their own networks, not by third-party CDN edge networks. Baker acknowledges CDNs have a potential future path toward becoming meaningful token path infrastructure and are already commanding premium pricing for low-latency AI delivery, but characterizes their current role as non-core. The investment implication is cautious: CDN stocks like Cloudflare, Fastly, and Akamai may be pricing in a token path narrative that is not yet supported by actual token delivery volumes, making the thesis contrarian against bullish CDN-as-AI-infrastructure narratives over the medium term.

source: TBPN — SpaceX IPO, token path, sovereign AI won't reach frontier
summary: China's AI advantage via distillation of Western frontier models is eroding as labs lock down reasoning traces and stop releasing frontier models openly
summary_extended: Baker argues that China's AI competitive advantage — built on industrial-scale distillation of Western frontier models — is structurally eroding as leading Western labs move to lock down their reasoning traces and cease open releases of frontier models. He cites DeepSeek as the canonical example, noting it was built from approximately 160,000 reasoning traces derived from OpenAI's o1/o3 models, and that Chinese actors run roughly 100,000 API endpoints continuously distilling from available Western models. The core thesis is that this distillation arbitrage only works while frontier models remain accessible, and that a shift toward closed, proprietary releases (he references a model called 'Mythos' as an early signal) will cut off this vector. The investment implication is that Western frontier AI labs — which are predominantly private — retain durable moats if they successfully close off distillation pathways, while the structural catch-up dynamic that made Chinese AI appear competitive may prove more fragile than consensus assumes. Baker presents no directly actionable public equity trade, as the primary actors (OpenAI, Anthropic, DeepSeek) are all private.

source: TBPN — SpaceX IPO, token path, sovereign AI won't reach frontier
summary: EV/net PP&E is an increasingly important valuation metric; installed physical assets on Earth will appreciate in value
summary_extended: Baker argues that EV/net PP&E is an increasingly important valuation metric for the current market environment, reflecting a structural view that installed physical assets on Earth will appreciate in value over time. He references Goldman Sachs's 'halo trade' concept — companies with high asset value and low obsolescence risk — as the intellectual framework underpinning this view. The implication is that asset-heavy AI infrastructure companies (hyperscalers, data center REITs, and similar capital-intensive businesses) are undervalued when assessed on this metric relative to their true replacement-cost value. Baker frames this as a contrarian lens: the market has historically rewarded asset-light businesses, but the AI infrastructure buildout is reversing that dynamic, making physical capital intensity a feature rather than a bug. Investors should look for companies with large, hard-to-replicate installed bases of physical infrastructure that face low technological obsolescence risk.

source: TBPN — SpaceX IPO, token path, sovereign AI won't reach frontier
summary: Being a 'token factory' is sufficient as a business model for the next 5-10 years; the world needs more token factories
summary_extended: Baker argues that being a 'token factory' — a pure-play AI compute provider focused solely on inference and training throughput — is a fully sufficient and durable business model for the next 5 to 10 years, without needing to build out a full cloud stack including databases, storage, or other services like AWS offers. His reasoning is structural: global demand for AI tokens will be so large that dedicated compute providers will be capacity-constrained for the foreseeable future, making vertical integration into adjacent cloud services unnecessary and potentially distracting. The investment implication is that pure-play AI compute and neocloud providers should not be penalized for lacking full-stack cloud capabilities, and that their focused model is a feature rather than a limitation. Baker frames this as a contrarian defense of the neocloud business model against critics who argue these companies need to become more like hyperscalers to survive long-term.

summary: Meta's EV/net PP&E multiple reflects extreme market skepticism about monetizing its asset base; outcome uncertain but Zuckerberg has proven ability to pivot
summary_extended: Baker observes that Meta's EV/net PP&E multiple is sitting at a level that implies the market has 'immense skepticism' about the company's ability to monetize its massive GPU and data center asset base. He points to specific evidence supporting that skepticism: Meta AI has not broken through as a top consumer app, there is no clear killer feature emerging within Instagram, and the API/enterprise business model remains undefined. However, Baker tempers this bearish read by noting Zuckerberg's demonstrated willingness to execute sharp pivots — citing the aggressive 2022 layoff cycle as evidence — and flags Muse Spark as an unexpected positive surprise that suggests upside optionality exists. Baker stops short of a bullish call, framing Meta as a 'we'll see' situation where the valuation reflects deep skepticism but the outcome is genuinely uncertain. The investment implication is that Meta is not a high-conviction long, but the depressed multiple relative to its physical asset base creates optionality worth monitoring rather than dismissing outright.

source: TBPN — SpaceX IPO, token path, sovereign AI won't reach frontier
summary: SpaceX near-term value is driven by terrestrial compute gigawatt ramp and Grok/cursor adoption, not long-duration space optionality
summary_extended: Baker argues that the near-term investment thesis for SpaceX is not about Mars colonization or asteroid mining, but rather two concrete near-term drivers: (1) the speed at which SpaceX can bring terrestrial compute gigawatts online, which Baker values at approximately $50B per gigawatt based on the Google deal — roughly half the 2-3x neocloud pricing premium cited by Altimeter — and (2) the adoption of Grok/Cursor, which Baker expects to reach half of the Fortune 500. He explicitly frames these two variables as what will matter most for SpaceX's valuation over the next year, making the long-duration space optionality (Mars, asteroid mining) a secondary consideration for investors.
