SpaceX remains private, so there is no direct public equity play, but the thesis has implications for how neocloud and AI infrastructure peers are priced relative to SpaceX's emerging compute business.

source: TBPN — SpaceX IPO, token path, sovereign AI won't reach frontier
summary: Sovereign AI for most countries will be fine-tuned open-source models run in local data centers, not frontier model development; China will fall further behind
summary_extended: Baker argues that sovereign AI for virtually all countries outside the US (and possibly China) will not involve developing frontier models, but will instead consist of fine-tuning open-source models on local language, culture, and values using RL, adding system prompts and supervised fine-tuning, and running the resulting models in local data centers. He contends that frontier-level sovereign AI development is structurally unviable for these nations due to the prohibitive cost and complexity involved. On China specifically, Baker believes Beijing is making a 'terrible mistake' by relying on domestic chips and distillation of Western frontier models, because as those frontier models get locked down (citing 'Mythos' as an example), China's distillation advantage will disappear and it will fall further behind. The investment implication is structural: open-source model providers and local/regional data center operators in non-US countries stand to benefit, but Baker names no specific publicly traded companies as direct plays. No specific numbers or timeframes are cited beyond a medium-term horizon.

summary: Anthropic is worth approximately $3 trillion as a public company today
summary_extended: Baker argues that Anthropic, if it were publicly traded today, would command a market capitalization of approximately $3 trillion, making it one of the most valuable companies in the world. His reasoning is grounded in projected 2025 revenues well above $100 billion, a strong growth trajectory toward $200-300 billion in revenue by 2028, and approximately 85% gross margins on inference at scale — a combination he views as justifying an extremely high valuation multiple. Baker frames this not as a supply/demand distortion from private market dynamics but as a genuine reflection of intrinsic value that public markets would also recognize. The investment implication is primarily observational given Anthropic's private status, but Baker's view signals that AI model providers operating at scale with high gross margins represent a new category of hyper-valued software asset. No directly investable US-listed equity is the subject of this thesis, as Anthropic remains private with no publicly traded pure-play equivalent.

source: All-In Podcast — E278: AI memory crunch, Micron blowout, NYC socialists, SpaceX float (w/ Kalanick)
summary: Chinese AI models (GLM 5.2) have achieved near-frontier capability through distillation of US model outputs, challenging the assumption of a durable US lead
summary_extended: Baker argues that Chinese AI labs have achieved near-frontier model capability through a systematic distillation process, specifically citing GLM 5.2 as a model that has 'challenged some of his beliefs' about the durability of the US AI lead. The mechanism he describes involves Chinese operators running large-scale iPhone farms to query US model APIs (such as OpenAI and Anthropic), harvesting reasoning traces at scale and using them for reinforcement learning training — effectively bootstrapping frontier capability from US model outputs. Critically, Baker notes that GLM 5.2 is now capable enough to conduct its own RL training, meaning the distillation flywheel may accelerate without continued dependence on US APIs. He estimates China is approximately 9 months behind US frontier models (plus or minus 3 months), a gap he views as narrower and more dynamic than previously assumed, with implications for the assumed moat of US AI incumbents.

source: All-In Podcast — E278: AI memory crunch, Micron blowout, NYC socialists, SpaceX float (w/ Kalanick)
summary: DRAM is the single most important bottleneck in AI infrastructure, not other components
summary_extended: Baker argues that DRAM is the single most critical bottleneck in AI infrastructure, explicitly dismissing competing bottleneck candidates including lasers, capacitors, power supply semiconductors, NAND flash, and HDDs. His reasoning is that memory capacity and bandwidth are foundational constraints that directly limit the performance of every AI model, making DRAM uniquely irreplaceable among hardware components. He cites Elon Musk's strategic focus on memory for Terapab as corroborating evidence that sophisticated AI builders recognize DRAM as the binding constraint. The investment implication is to be long DRAM producers, with Micron (MU) being the primary US-listed beneficiary; SK Hynix and Samsung are the other major producers but are not US-listed equities. No specific price targets or timeframes beyond 'medium-term' were cited.

source: All-In Podcast — E278: AI memory crunch, Micron blowout, NYC socialists, SpaceX float (w/ Kalanick)
summary: Terrestrial data center capacity that does get built will become extremely valuable ('hope diamonds') as contestation rates rise
summary_extended: Baker argues that the accelerating contestation and blocking of planned data center projects — which he estimates has affected roughly 40% of projects since 2021 — will make successfully permitted and energized terrestrial compute capacity extraordinarily scarce and valuable, likening it to 'hope diamonds.' He reasons that as opposition from local communities, regulators, and grid operators intensifies, the rate of successful project completion will decline further, creating a structural supply constraint on large-scale (hundreds of megawatts to gigawatt-class) data center capacity. The investment implication is that operators who already hold permitted, energized, and operational terrestrial data center capacity will command significant pricing power and valuation premiums. Baker implies investors should favor established, operating data center REITs and hyperscale operators with existing capacity over those dependent on new greenfield development pipelines.

source: All-In Podcast — E278: AI memory crunch, Micron blowout, NYC socialists, SpaceX float (w/ Kalanick)
summary: Disaggregation of inference into prefill and decode phases will extend the useful life of older Nvidia GPUs (H100s, A100s) by 7-12 years
summary_extended: Baker argues that the disaggregation of inference workloads into two distinct phases — prefill (memory-capacity-bound) and decode (memory-bandwidth-bound) — fundamentally changes how AI infrastructure should be architected and valued. By placing specialized decode-optimized chips such as those from Groq and Cerebras in front of older Nvidia H100 and A100 GPUs, operators can build competitive inference stacks without requiring the latest-generation hardware, effectively extending the useful economic life of existing GPU assets by 7 to 12 years. This is a contrarian view relative to the prevailing assumption that AI capex cycles are short and that older GPUs become obsolete quickly; Baker contends the opposite, that H100s and A100s remain highly relevant for prefill tasks. The investment implication is that AI capex becomes more financeable and that companies holding large inventories of older Nvidia GPUs retain significant asset value, while specialized inference chip vendors like Cerebras (CBRS) become critical infrastructure components rather than niche players.

source: Generating Alpha — Ep.56: career arc, semis, NVDA/Tesla, AI infra cycles
summary: We are not in a valuation bubble in tech; multiples have compressed and tech now trades at a discount to staples
summary_extended: Baker argues that the widespread narrative of a tech valuation bubble is factually incorrect and reflects a lack of attention to actual market data. He states that tech multiples are at the same level they were five to six years ago, having compressed meaningfully since early 2025, and that tech now trades at a discount to consumer staples — a historically rare and notable occurrence. His reasoning is that bubble-callers are conflating high absolute prices or strong performance with stretched valuations, when in reality the earnings growth of tech companies has kept pace with or exceeded price appreciation. The investment implication is that tech broadly is not overvalued and may in fact represent relative value versus defensive sectors, suggesting investors should not underweight tech on valuation grounds alone.

source: Generating Alpha — Ep.56: career arc, semis, NVDA/Tesla, AI infra cycles
summary: AI world models will reduce AAA game development costs by ~90%, benefiting platform holders and harming game developers
summary_extended: Baker argues that AI world models will reduce the cost of developing AAA video games by approximately 90%, collapsing per-title budgets from the current $200-300M range and triggering a massive explosion in content supply. The core reasoning is that AI-generated world models will automate much of the labor-intensive asset creation, environment design, and testing that currently drives development costs, making it feasible for smaller teams to produce high-quality titles. This dynamic is structurally negative for incumbent AAA game studios, which will face intensified competition from a flood of lower-cost entrants, compressing margins and eroding pricing power. Conversely, Baker views video game platform holders as the primary beneficiaries, since content abundance increases the value proposition of their platforms and drives engagement and subscriber growth. No specific timeline was given, but the implication is a medium-term structural shift that favors platform economics over content creator economics.

source: Generating Alpha — Ep.56: career arc, semis, NVDA/Tesla, AI infra cycles
summary: As AI eats the world, silicon eats the world — semiconductor intensity of AI is structurally higher than deterministic software
summary_extended: Baker argues that the shift from deterministic software to probabilistic AI represents a structural and permanent increase in semiconductor intensity across the economy. Unlike traditional software, which executes fixed logic, AI models recompute probabilistic answers each time they run, making them orders of magnitude more computationally expensive per task. This creates a durable, secular tailwind for the semiconductor industry broadly — not a cyclical upturn — and Baker frames this as his foundational investment thesis and 'first love' as an investor. The implication is that semiconductor exposure should be treated as a long-term structural position, not a trade, as AI adoption compounds the demand for compute across every sector of the economy. He does not cite specific numbers but emphasizes the permanence and breadth of the trend using the phrase 'as AI eats the world, silicon is going to eat the world.'

summary: AI rendering of video games on-device (replacing local GPU) is not viable in the next 5-7 years
summary_extended: Baker explicitly dismisses as 'ridiculous' the thesis that AI cloud rendering will replace local GPU rendering on smartphones, iPads, and PCs within the next 5-7 years. His core argument is economic: using a cloud AI model like VO3 to render a game such as Monopoly Go at current list prices would cost more than two orders of magnitude more than the game's entire revenue, making the unit economics completely unworkable at any realistic scale. The implication is that local GPU silicon — the chips inside phones, tablets, and PCs that handle graphics rendering today — will not be displaced by AI inference in the near-to-medium term. This is a bearish view on a speculative AI disruption thesis rather than a bullish call on any specific company, though it implicitly supports the continued relevance of on-device GPU vendors. Investors should be skeptical of any narrative claiming AI cloud rendering will cannibalize local GPU demand within this timeframe.

source: Generating Alpha — Ep.56: career arc, semis, NVDA/Tesla, AI infra cycles
summary: Watts and wafers shortage may prevent an AI overbuild/bubble, alongside the psychological scars of the dot-com bubble
summary_extended: Baker argues that two structural physical constraints — a shortage of power (watts) available for data center buildout and a shortage of semiconductor manufacturing capacity (wafers, primarily at TSMC) — will act as natural governors preventing the AI infrastructure cycle from becoming a true bubble or overbuild. Unlike the dot-com era, where capital could flow freely into fiber and networking buildout, the AI cycle is gated by real-world physical limits that cannot be quickly resolved regardless of capital availability. Baker also cites the psychological scarring of dot-com-era investors as an additional behavioral brake on excess speculation. The investment implication is that AI infrastructure spending is more likely to be supply-constrained than demand-constrained, meaning the cycle could be longer and more durable than skeptics expect, with less risk of a catastrophic overbuild collapse. No specific price targets or timeframes are cited, but Baker frames this as a medium-term structural view he holds with high conviction.
